entries.prompt: Walk me through a moment from the past few days that's still replaying in your head — what happened, who was there, and what you keep coming back to about it.
entries.content:
Tuesday afternoon, the review meeting. Mei was there, and Devon, and two people from the other team whose names I always have to think about for a second. We were going through the deck I'd put together on Monday night. I was a little proud of it, honestly. I'd stayed up later than I meant to, and there was a section in the middle I thought was actually clean — I'd cut a slide I knew Mei would have cut, and I felt smug about catching it before she did.

Then we got to slide nine and Mei said, "Wait, is this the old number?"

And it was. It was the old number. I'd updated everything else and left that one chart with the figure from two weeks ago. Off by like fourteen percent. Not catastrophic. Nobody was going to make a decision on that single chart. But it was wrong, and Mei caught it, and she said it in that even voice she has where you can't tell if she's annoyed or just clarifying.

I said, "Oh — yeah, good catch, let me fix that after." Calm. Professional. I think I even smiled a little, the small self-deprecating one. Devon made some joke about how charts are always the thing, and we moved on.

And then for the rest of the meeting I was somewhere else. Not gone, exactly — I was still answering questions, I think reasonably — but underneath there was this loud thing going *you are so careless, this is the kind of thing you used to catch in your sleep, what is wrong with you, she's going to think you're sloppy now, she's going to remember this.* Like a kid who got a problem wrong on the board. Eight years old and standing there.

The meeting ended. Devon said something funny on the way out. I laughed. I went back to my desk and fixed the chart in about ninety seconds and sent the corrected deck around with a quick "updated slide 9 — sorry for the churn!" and Mei replied with a thumbs up within a minute.

A thumbs up. That's it. That's the whole consequence.

And I have been replaying this for two days.

What I keep coming back to is the gap. The gap between what actually happened — a small error, caught, fixed, forgotten by everyone except me — and what it felt like, which was being found out. Like the chart was evidence of something larger. Like Mei now had a data point and she was filing it away.

I know she isn't. I know that. If Devon had done the same thing I would have thought *oh, charts, classic* and not given it another thought. I extend that to him without trying. I cannot extend it to myself.

The other thing I keep coming back to is that I was *proud* of the deck going in. That's the part that stings most, I think. I had this small private feeling of *I did a good job on this* and the chart error landed right on top of it and now I can't access the proud feeling at all. It's been overwritten. Two days of work and the only thing my brain wants to remember is the fourteen percent.

Sam noticed something Tuesday night. I was on my phone for a long time on the couch and he said, "You okay? You've been quiet." And I said yeah, just a long day, and he let it go, which I appreciated and also was a little disappointed by, which is unfair of me. I didn't want to talk about it because saying it out loud would have made me hear how small it was. *Mei caught a wrong number on slide nine and I'm spiraling.* I can hear myself saying it. I can hear how it would sound.

And yet here I am at the kitchen table on Thursday still turning it over.

I think part of what's happening is that I want Mei to think I'm good. Not good enough — good. There's some specific weight her opinion carries that nobody else's does, and I don't fully know why. She's only a couple years ahead of me. She's not my manager. But when she said "is this the old number" something in me went *oh no, she sees you now, she sees the real version.*

That phrase. *The real version.* As if the careful version is the fake one and the one who misses a chart is the true one underneath. That's not how anyone else seems to operate. Devon is just Devon whether he's nailing it or fumbling it. He doesn't seem to have a real version waiting to be discovered.

I don't know what to do with this except to write it down, which I guess is what I'm doing. I am not going to bring it up with Mei. There's nothing to bring up. I am going to keep working with her and the chart will eventually be a thing I forget, probably by next Tuesday, and something else will take its place.

But I'd like to remember, when the next one comes, that the consequence was a thumbs up. That the whole external event was: error, catch, fix, thumbs up. That the rest of it — the eight-year-old at the board, the *what is wrong with you* — is happening in a room only I am in. Mei is not in that room. Nobody is. I built it and I'm the only one standing in it, talking to myself like that.

I keep thinking I'll remember next time. I keep thinking that.

entries.prompt: Think about something you said yes to recently — a favor, a meeting, a plan — that you weren't fully on board with. Take me back to the moment of agreeing: what was the other person's face doing, what did you almost say instead, and what made you land on yes?
entries.content:
Friday, late, kitchen table again.

It was Devon, actually. Wednesday morning, by the coffee machine. He said, "Hey, are you around Saturday afternoon? A few of us are going to that thing in the park, the food market thing, you should come."

His face was easy. That's the thing about Devon, his face is always easy. He wasn't asking like it mattered. He was asking the way you ask someone if they want the last slice — genuinely fine either way. There was no pressure on it. Which somehow makes what I did next worse, or at least less explainable.

I said "yeah, sure, that sounds fun" before I had finished pouring the coffee.

What I almost said — what I had actually been thinking about thirty seconds before he asked, while I was waiting for the machine — was that I wanted Saturday to be nothing. Sam has a thing in the morning and I had this whole picture of the apartment to myself for a few hours, the good kind of quiet, maybe finally opening the book that's been on the nightstand giving me a guilty look for three weeks. I had been looking forward to it in that low, private way you look forward to small things. And then Devon said park, and I said yes, and I watched the small private thing evaporate in real time while I was still stirring milk in.

What made me land on yes. I've been sitting with this for an hour and I think it's a few things stacked.

One is that it was Devon and I like Devon and the easiness of him makes me want to match it. Saying no would have required some small explanation, some "oh I've got a quiet weekend planned," and the quiet weekend would have sounded, out loud, like nothing. Like I was protecting nothing. *I can't, I'm reading.* I can hear how that lands. Devon would have said totally, no worries, and meant it, and I still couldn't make myself say it.

Two is that I think there's a tally somewhere in my head of how many times I've said no to social things lately and I am aware the number is getting high. Not high in any way other people would notice. High in the way I notice. There's a low hum of *you're becoming the person who doesn't come to things* and Wednesday morning I think I was paying a small tax against that hum.

Three — and this is the one I don't love seeing — is that Devon is friends with Mei. They go to things together. If I went on Saturday, Mei might be there. And there's some calculation, half a layer down, that says being seen at a casual thing, being easy and fun in front of her, would be useful. Would smooth something. Would put a different data point next to the chart one. I notice I am doing this and I notice I do not like that I am doing this. Saturday afternoon in the park is not a referendum on slide nine. But apparently some part of me is trying to make it one.

The yes happened in maybe two seconds and contained all three of those things, which is wild to write down. It felt, in the moment, like just being friendly.

And then the rest of Wednesday I had this small low resentment, which is the thing I always do. I resented Devon a little, which is unfair because he asked completely cleanly. I resented the park thing for existing. I sat at my desk at 4pm and thought *I don't want to go on Saturday* and then thought *well, you said you would.* Like the version of me from 9am had signed a contract the version of me at 4pm had to honor.

The maddening thing is I know how this ends. I'll go. I'll probably have an okay time. I might even have a good time — these things are usually fine once I'm there, and Devon is genuinely fun, and the food will be good. And I will come home Saturday evening and feel that specific tiredness of having spent social energy I didn't quite want to spend, and Sunday will feel shorter because of it, and by Monday I'll have forgotten I ever wanted the quiet afternoon at all. The original want gets erased. It always gets erased. Which I think is part of why it's so easy to say yes — some part of me knows the wanting will disappear under the doing, so the wanting doesn't get to vote.

I texted Sam about it earlier, half complaining, and he said "you can just not go" and I said "I already said yes" and he said "people cancel." Which, yes, true. People cancel. I am not going to cancel. Canceling would require a second small lie or a real explanation and the real explanation is *I wanted to read a book by myself* and I can't seem to make that a sentence I can give a person.

I notice I do this with bigger things too. The yes that comes out before the wanting has a chance. Riley asked months ago if I'd come visit and I said yes immediately and then have, somehow, not booked a flight, which is its own kind of answer I haven't given her. At least with Devon I'll actually show up.

What I would like — and I'm putting this here knowing I probably won't do it — is to have a five-second pause. Just five seconds, between a question and my answer. Long enough to check whether the yes is mine or whether it's the hum's. Long enough that *I think I'm going to take Saturday quiet* could be a sentence I get to.

Five seconds. I keep thinking I'll remember next time.

I keep thinking that.

entries.prompt: The last two entries have been about moments where something went sideways — the wrong chart, the reluctant yes. Try a different angle: when was the last time you were genuinely proud of how you handled something, and what did it look like from the inside while it was happening?
entries.content:
Sunday night. Park thing was yesterday and it was fine, by the way, exactly as predicted, and I did come home tired and Sunday did feel short. Filing that away.

But the prompt is asking about something else, and I had to actually sit here for a few minutes to find one, which is itself a thing worth noticing. My brain did not produce a recent proud moment on demand. It produced three more sideways moments first. I had to push past those.

Here's what I landed on. Two weeks ago, maybe a little more. The Thursday standup. We were going around on status and one of the people from the other team — I'll just say K — was presenting some early thinking on a project that overlaps with ours. And the thinking had a problem in it. Not a small one. It was built on an assumption that, if you carried it forward two months, was going to make a real mess for our side. I could see it while K was still talking.

The old move, the reflex move, would have been to say nothing in the meeting and then go stew about it at my desk and then maybe send Mei a Slack like *did you catch the thing with K's plan* and let her be the one to raise it. Outsource the friction. I have done this exact thing many times. I know the shape of it.

What I did instead was wait until K finished, and then I said, "Can I ask about the assumption on the timing — I think if that shifts, the whole back half changes, and I want to make sure I'm reading it right." Something like that. I phrased it as a question because I wasn't a hundred percent sure I was right and also because I didn't want to sandbag K in front of everyone. K paused, thought about it, and said, "Oh. Yeah. That's actually a good point, let me come back on that." And we moved on.

That's it. That's the whole moment. From the outside it was thirty seconds of meeting.

What I want to try to get down is what it was like from the inside, because I think that's the part I'd lose otherwise.

There was a beat, maybe ten seconds, between when I saw the problem and when I spoke. In that beat I had the full menu. I had *don't say it, you might be wrong, you'll look like you're showing off, K will be embarrassed, this isn't your project, who do you think you are.* All of it. The whole chorus. I want to be honest that the chorus was loud. It wasn't that I was serene and just confidently raised a point. I was scared in a low background way, the way you're scared right before you raise your hand in a class where you don't usually raise your hand.

And then there was a different thing underneath the chorus, quieter, that just said: *if you don't say this now, in two months it'll be worse, and you'll have known.* That sentence — *you'll have known* — is what got me to open my mouth. Not bravery exactly. More like a refusal to be the person who saw it and stayed quiet to stay comfortable.

The phrasing happened almost on its own. I did not script it. I think because I had been in enough of these meetings to know how to ask a question that's actually a flag without making it feel like a flag, I had the muscle for it. That's a thing I want to give myself credit for and it's hard to. The muscle is real. It came from years of meetings. It is not nothing.

After I said it, while K was thinking, there was a half second where I was sure I had been wrong, where the chorus came back loud — *oh god you said it and you're wrong, everyone's looking, Mei is looking* — and then K said yeah, good point, and the room moved on, and I felt this small clean feeling. Not triumph. Not even pride exactly, in the moment. Just — accurate. Like I had matched what I did to what I saw, and the gap between inside and outside had closed for thirty seconds.

That's the part I want to remember. The gap closing. Most of the time the gap is wide — the calm exterior, the loud interior, the careful sentence and the eight-year-old. In that moment they were the same size. The thing I thought and the thing I said were the same thing. That is rarer than I want it to be.

Mei messaged me afterward. Just "good catch on K's thing." Three words. And I noticed — I want to be honest about this — that I was a little high on it for the rest of the afternoon. Which is its own small embarrassing data point about how much her three words can move me. But I'm trying to write about the proud part, not the Mei part, and the proud part actually predates her message. The proud part was the thirty seconds in the room. I felt it before she said anything. That matters. That's mine.

What I notice writing this is how much harder it is to stay with than the chart story. The chart story has grooves worn in it; my brain knows the route. This one I keep wanting to undercut. *It wasn't that big a deal. Anyone would have said it. K probably would've caught it themselves.* I can feel the pull to dismantle it even now, three paragraphs in.

So let me just say the plain thing and stop. I saw something, I was scared to say it, I said it anyway, in a way that was useful and not unkind, and it landed. That happened. I did that.

I would like to remember this one too. I keep thinking I'll remember.

entries.prompt: Is there something small you've been telling yourself you'll get to — an email, a errand, a conversation — that keeps not happening? Describe the most recent moment you remembered it and then didn't do it. What were you doing instead, and what was the thought that let you slide past it?
entries.content:
Tuesday night. The flight to Riley.

I remembered it at 9:47pm. I know the time because I had just looked at my phone to check something else — I think to see if a delivery had moved — and there in the notifications was a text from Riley from earlier in the day. Not about the visit. Just a picture of her dog wearing a small ridiculous hat. Caption: *current mood.* I laughed. I sent back a string of emojis. And while I was typing the emojis some other channel in my head opened up and said *you still haven't booked the flight.*

What I was doing was lying on the couch with Sam, half-watching something neither of us was really watching, scrolling. The laptop was three feet away on the coffee table. Three feet. I could have leaned forward. The whole thing — pulling up the airline site, picking a weekend, putting in the card — is maybe twelve minutes of actual work. I have done it for other trips, faster. I once booked a flight to my parents' in under five minutes from this exact couch.

The thought that let me slide past it was: *I'll do it tomorrow when I can actually look at my calendar properly.*

Which is a beautiful sentence. It sounds responsible. It sounds like the considered version of yes. *Properly.* As if booking a flight requires some sacred alignment of conditions I cannot summon at 9:47pm. As if I haven't been saying *tomorrow when I can look at my calendar properly* for — and I had to actually count this — something like four months. Riley asked in, I think, June. It is now October.

Four months.

I put the phone face down on my chest and went back to the show. Sam said something about the character on screen and I laughed at the right place. Inside I was doing this small fast thing where I was simultaneously aware that I had just remembered the flight and chosen not to do it, and also already constructing the version of the story where I hadn't quite chosen, where it had just sort of slipped past again, accidentally. Like the choice happened in some passive voice.

It did not. I made it. I was holding the phone.

What I keep getting stuck on is that I want to see Riley. That's the part that doesn't fit. This isn't the Devon situation, where the yes was misaligned with what I actually wanted. I actually want to go. I miss her. We have been friends for eleven years and there is a specific kind of conversation I only have with her and I can feel the lack of it in my week. So why is my hand not moving the twelve inches to the laptop.

I think — and I'm trying to be honest here even though I don't love the shape of it — I think it's that booking the flight would make it real, and making it real would mean the four months would also become real. Right now the four months are sort of suspended. As long as I haven't booked it, I'm someone who is going to visit Riley. The minute I book it, the dates make the delay visible. *Oh, you're coming in November? It's been almost half a year since I asked.* She wouldn't say that. Riley would never say that. She would just be happy. But I would hear it anyway, in my own head, in that voice that is harsher than anyone else's voice.

There's also — and this is the smaller, dumber thing — a kind of conversation I owe her that's been growing while I haven't booked. The longer I take, the more the not-booking becomes the thing, instead of the visit being the thing. I have gone from someone who hadn't gotten around to it yet to someone who is conspicuously not getting around to it. And the gap between those two is hard to acknowledge without acknowledging that I have been, in some small way, avoiding her. Not her, exactly. Avoiding the moment of contact that booking would create. Which is — when I write it out — pretty close to avoiding her.

I would like to call Riley. I would like to call her tonight, even, and not even mention the flight, just talk for an hour the way we used to. But that has its own version of the same problem. The longer it's been since I really talked to her, the bigger the call gets in my head, until it is no longer a call you make on a Tuesday on the couch but some kind of summit that requires preparation. And so I text her dog photos. And she texts me dog photos. And we keep the channel barely open with the lowest-bandwidth signal possible, and I tell myself we're in touch.

The thought that let me slide past it. *I'll do it tomorrow when I can actually look at my calendar properly.* I want to write that down clean so I can recognize it the next time it shows up, which it will, probably tomorrow. That sentence is not a plan. It is a way of getting the present moment off the hook by handing the bill to a future version of me who has, so far, not paid it once.

I am not going to open the laptop right now. I want to say I will, to make this entry have a satisfying ending, but I won't, and I'd rather not lie at the kitchen table. What I am going to try is smaller. Tomorrow morning, before standup, I am going to put two specific weekends in a text to Riley. Not book anything. Just send her two weekends. *Either of these work for you?* That's the whole task. Maybe ninety seconds.

Ninety seconds. I keep thinking I'll do it.

I keep thinking that.

entries.prompt: When was the last time someone gave you a compliment or thanks that didn't quite land — that you brushed off, or argued with internally, or accepted out loud while disagreeing in your head? Take me back to the exchange: what did they say, and what was the counter-argument running underneath your response?
entries.content:
Wednesday night. And yes, before I get to the prompt — I did send the text to Riley this morning. Two weekends. She wrote back within twenty minutes with one of them circled and a string of exclamation points. So that happened. I want to note it without making a whole thing of it. It happened. Moving on.

The compliment. This is from Monday, and I've been carrying it around in a low-grade way ever since.

It was Mei, which of course it was. We had wrapped a working session — just the two of us, going through some structure stuff for a thing we're co-owning — and as we were closing laptops she said, "You're really good at this, you know. The structural part. I always feel like things get clearer when you're the one organizing them."

That was the sentence. Verbatim, or close to it. *Things get clearer when you're the one organizing them.*

I said, "Oh — thanks, that's nice of you to say. I think it's just because I've been staring at this kind of thing for a while."

That was my mouth. What was happening underneath my mouth was a small fast argument with everything she had just said.

The argument went something like: *she's being generous because we just had a good session and she's in a good mood. She's saying that because I caught the structure thing today but she hasn't forgotten the chart from three weeks ago. This is the kind of thing senior people say to peers to be encouraging — it doesn't mean she actually thinks it, it means she knows the social move. And anyway "good at the structural part" is a small compliment, it's not "good," it's good-at-one-thing, which is sort of the polite version of being told you have one usable skill.*

All of that, in maybe two seconds, while I was saying *that's nice of you to say.*

I want to write that out because I don't think I usually let myself see how fast and how thorough the dismantling is. She said one warm sentence. I had a four-part counter-argument ready before I had finished my reply. Four parts. About a compliment.

And then — this is the part I really want to get down — I spent the walk home re-litigating it. Was she being sincere. Was the phrasing slightly hedged. *Really good at this* versus *really good.* The "this." The qualifier. Was the qualifier doing work. Was she narrowing it on purpose because she didn't want to overclaim. Did *I always feel like things get clearer* mean she had previously felt things were unclear, and if so, when, and was that about me, and —

I want to stop typing that paragraph because writing it out is making me feel a little sick. That is a lot of machinery to run on one sentence from a coworker who was being kind.

The thing I keep getting caught on is: if Devon had told me Mei said that to him, I would have immediately thought *oh, that's a real compliment, Mei doesn't say things she doesn't mean.* I would have been a little jealous of him, even. I have, in fact, been mildly jealous of small warm things Mei has said about Devon's work in passing. So I clearly believe Mei means what she says when the recipient is anyone other than me. The disbelief is specifically reserved for compliments aimed at this seat.

There's a sentence I have been turning over since Monday and I'll just write it down. *I cannot let her be right about me.* Something like that. If Mei thinks I'm good at the structural part, and Mei is reliable about these things, then I would have to walk around being someone who is, demonstrably, good at the structural part. And I cannot quite hold that. There's some load-bearing wall in here that requires me to be slightly worse than people think, so that when the chart error happens, the chart error is the truth coming out, and the compliment was the misperception. If I let the compliment be the truth, then the chart was just a chart, and I would have to — I don't know. Be less braced. Walk around less braced.

Less braced is sort of terrifying to imagine. I have been braced for a long time. Bracing is the posture. If I'm not braced, what am I.

I notice I'm getting abstract and I want to come back to the moment. Mei, laptop closing, that sentence. Her face was — neutral, I think, the way her face usually is, but with a small warm edge on it. She wasn't performing. She was packing up her bag. It was an offhand thing, said while doing something else, which is actually how Mei says things she means. The performative version of Mei is more careful. The offhand version is the real one. I know this. I have observed this about her for two years.

So the most accurate read of Monday is: a person who is careful with her words, in the offhand register she uses for things she actually thinks, told me I make things clearer. And I said thanks and immediately went to work proving she was wrong.

What I would like — and I am trying to write this without it becoming another *I keep thinking I'll remember* — is to just let the sentence sit somewhere I can find it. Not argue with it. Not inflate it either. Just: she said that. On Monday. While packing up her laptop. Things get clearer when you're the one organizing them.

I'm going to leave it there. I'm not going to add the qualifier I can feel forming.

The sentence was the sentence. She said it. That's the entry.

entries.prompt: When was the last time you caught yourself performing a version of yourself that didn't quite match what was going on inside? Take me into the room — who were you with, what were you doing with your face and your voice, and what was the gap between the outside and the inside?
entries.content:
Thursday night. Late. Sam's asleep.

Today, lunch. Mei and Devon and I went to the place down the block, the one with the counter. This is a thing we do maybe once every two weeks, the three of us. Devon engineered it months ago and now it just kind of happens.

We were sitting at the counter waiting for food and Devon was telling a story about his weekend — something about his sister's apartment and a piece of furniture that wouldn't fit through the door. He's a good storyteller. He commits to the dumb parts. Mei was laughing in the way she laughs, which is mostly with her shoulders, and I was laughing too, and at some point I caught myself from a kind of half-step outside and watched what my face was doing.

My face was doing the right thing. Eyebrows up at the right beat. Laugh on the punchline, not before. Head tilted slightly toward Devon, which is the listening tilt, the one that says *I'm with you, keep going.* My voice, when I added the small follow-up — "wait, so it's still in the hallway?" — was warm and curious in exactly the register the moment called for.

And underneath it I was somewhere else entirely. I was running a low background calculation about an email I had not answered before lunch. And underneath *that* I was tracking Mei's energy, whether she seemed tired today, whether the working session we have tomorrow was going to be the easy kind or the careful kind. And underneath *that* — and this is the part I am embarrassed to write — I was monitoring whether I was being fun enough. Whether my contributions to the conversation were landing. Whether Devon was getting a good lunch out of me. Whether Mei was.

So my face was doing one thing — easy, present, friend at lunch — and inside there were three or four open tabs, none of them about the furniture story.

Here's what got me. Devon finished the story and there was that natural pause where someone else picks up the thread, and Mei said something like, "okay but my real question is why you tried to move it yourself instead of paying someone," and Devon said something back, and they had this small easy exchange, and I was — for maybe four seconds — just a person at a counter listening to two other people talk. Not contributing. Not tracking. Just listening. And in those four seconds I felt the gap close a little. I was where my face was. The eyebrows and the inside matched.

And then the four seconds ended because some part of me noticed I hadn't said anything in a while and went *say something, you're being quiet, quiet reads as weird,* and I said something — I don't even remember what, something fine — and the tabs all opened back up.

Four seconds. That was the window.

I want to write down what's bothering me about this, because at first glance it's nothing. So I performed at lunch. Everyone performs at lunch. Lunch is mildly performed by definition, that's why we go to lunch instead of just eating at our desks. The performance is the social contract. Devon is performing too. Mei is, in her way, performing. Nobody at that counter was their unmediated three-a.m. self and nobody wanted anyone else to be.

But the gap I'm describing isn't the normal lunch gap. The normal lunch gap is small. The normal lunch gap is *I'm a little tired but I'm being lively.* What I was doing was bigger. I was running an entire surveillance operation on the meal while also conducting it. The face was on autopilot precisely because the rest of me was too busy to drive it.

And the autopilot is good. That's the thing I want to be honest about. The autopilot is well-trained. It produces correct laughs and warm follow-up questions and the occasional genuinely funny line. People like the autopilot. The autopilot is, in some ways, a more reliable lunch companion than the version of me that would be there if all the tabs were closed, because that version would sometimes go quiet for a whole minute and stare at her sandwich.

But I notice that I cannot fully tell, anymore, which parts of lunch-me are autopilot and which parts are me. That's the thing. If you asked me *did you enjoy lunch* I would say yes, and I would mean it, but I would be reporting the autopilot's data, not mine. The autopilot enjoyed lunch. I'm not sure where I was.

The four seconds of just listening are the closest I came to being there. And I shut them down myself because not contributing felt unsafe. Even though no one was grading me. Even though Devon and Mei were perfectly happy talking to each other for four whole seconds without my input.

The eight-year-old at the board again, kind of. Different shape, same kid. *Be on. Stay on. Don't let there be a quiet spot with your name on it.*

What I want to put down before I close the laptop is the small thing I noticed, the four seconds, because the four seconds are the data point. Not the whole lunch. The four seconds. That was a moment where the face and the inside were the same size, and I can find it again. It happened while I was listening. Not while I was producing.

That might be the actual finding. That the gap closes when I stop producing. Not when I produce something good — when I stop.

I'd like to try a longer version of those four seconds at the next lunch. Eight seconds, maybe. A whole sandwich's worth of just listening. See what happens.

I keep thinking I'll try.

I'm going to leave that one alone tonight and just go to bed.

entries.prompt: There's a kind of tiredness that isn't really about sleep — it's about how much of the day you spent being the version of you that other people needed. When did you feel that this week, and what would the un-performed version of that stretch of time have looked like instead?
entries.content:
Friday night. The good kind of Friday where nothing is required of me until Monday and I can feel my shoulders dropping by inches over the course of the evening.

The tiredness the prompt is asking about — I felt it most on Wednesday. Specifically Wednesday between about 10am and 4pm, which was a stretch of three meetings back to back to back with a forty-minute gap in the middle that I filled with Slack and a sad desk salad.

The first meeting was a kickoff with people from the other team, including the two whose names I always have to think about. The second was a one-on-one with Mei. The third was a larger thing where I had to present a piece of work to a group that included someone two levels up whose attention I want and don't want at the same time.

By the third meeting I had, without quite noticing, been on for about five hours. On meaning: warm voice, useful questions, the slight forward lean, the *yes, and* energy, the small jokes calibrated to the room. Different on for each meeting — the kickoff on is more open and curious, the Mei on is more crisp and prepared, the senior-person on is more measured and slightly slower. Three different costumes. Same body underneath.

When the third meeting ended I closed the laptop and just sat there for a minute. Not thinking anything in particular. Just — empty in a specific way. Not sleepy. Not even unhappy. Just used up. Like I had spent a currency I hadn't realized I was spending until the wallet was empty.

I went and made tea I didn't really want, which is a thing I do when I need a reason to walk away from my desk. Stood in the kitchen at the office. Watched the kettle. There's a window in there that looks out at the side of another building and I just looked at the building for a while.

That moment in the kitchen — the kettle and the building — was the closest thing to un-performed I got all day. Nobody in there. Nothing required. Just standing.

What would the un-performed version of those six hours have looked like. This is harder to write than I thought it would be, because I'm not sure I know.

The honest answer is I don't fully know what un-performed looks like for me at work. The kickoff meeting needs a version of me who is generous and engaged with strangers. The one-on-one with Mei needs a version of me who is sharp and prepared. The presentation needs a version of me who is calm and authoritative. There is no meeting on the calendar that wants the version of me who is tired and a little distracted and not sure about something. That version doesn't get invited.

So when I try to picture the un-performed Wednesday, I don't get a different Wednesday. I get a Wednesday with fewer meetings. Which isn't really an answer to the prompt. The prompt is asking what I would have done instead, and what I want to do instead is not be there, which feels like a cop-out.

Let me try again. Inside each of those meetings, what would have shifted if I'd been less on.

The kickoff: I would have asked one fewer question. Not because the questions were fake — they weren't, I was actually curious — but because some of them were curiosity and some of them were *make sure they see you're engaged.* The second kind is the on-tax. Drop those. Ask three real questions instead of five mixed ones.

The Mei one-on-one: I would have let a silence sit. There was a moment where she was thinking about something and I jumped in to fill the air, and what I filled it with was fine but not necessary. She would have gotten there. I didn't need to bridge the quiet for her. I bridged it for me. Drop that.

The presentation: I would have said *I'm not sure* once. There was a question from the senior person where my real answer was *I'd want to think about that more,* and what I said instead was a smooth thing that gestured at an answer without committing to one. Nobody was fooled, probably. But I performed having-an-answer instead of admitting not-quite-having-one. Drop that.

If I had dropped those three things — the extra question, the bridged silence, the false confidence — Wednesday would have been the same Wednesday from the outside, more or less, and I think I would have walked out of the third meeting with maybe forty percent more left in the tank. That's a guess. But it tracks with the kitchen. The kitchen was restful because nothing was being performed. The performance is the tax, not the meetings themselves.

What I notice writing this is that all three drops are small. None of them are *be a different person at work.* They're more like — let the seams show in places where the seams already show, and stop spackling them. The senior person knew I didn't fully have the answer. Mei could handle her own silence. The kickoff team did not need a fifth question to know I was paying attention.

The spackling is the work. Not the meetings. The meetings would happen anyway. The spackling is what I'm doing on top, constantly, and it is what was empty in the kitchen.

I don't think I'm going to walk into Monday and stop spackling. The spackling is old. But I could maybe pick one meeting next week and try the *I'm not sure* once. Just once. As an experiment. See what happens to the tank by 4pm.

Sam just came in to say he's going to bed. I told him I'd be in soon. I might actually be in soon, for once, instead of doing the phone-on-the-couch thing until midnight. The tea worked. The building helped. The kitchen at work and the kitchen at home, doing roughly the same job.

I'd like to find more kitchens.

entries.prompt: The last few entries have circled the same territory — the gap between your outside and inside, the performed warmth at lunch, the meetings as costumes. Step out of that for a second: when was the last time you were alone and surprised by what you actually wanted to do with the time? Take me into that stretch.
entries.content:
Saturday morning. Sam had the thing he had two weekends ago — same standing commitment, gone by 8:30, back around 1. So I had the apartment from 8:30 to 1, four and a half hours, the configuration I had wanted before Devon and the park.

I made coffee. Sat with it on the couch. Opened my phone, closed it, opened it, closed it. That fidgety thing where the hand reaches before the brain does. I put the phone in the other room, which is a trick I've read about and rolled my eyes at and apparently now do.

And then I sat there with the coffee and waited to find out what I wanted.

This is the part I want to get down, because it surprised me. I had assumed — based on weeks of telling myself I wanted a quiet morning to read — that the second I had a quiet morning, I would read. I had built it up. The book on the nightstand. The guilty look. The whole picture of myself curled up with it. I had narrated this future Saturday to myself maybe a dozen times.

I did not want to read. I sat there with the coffee and checked, the way you check a tooth with your tongue, and the wanting wasn't there. The book was just a book on a table in the other room. I could feel myself trying to manufacture the wanting — *come on, this is what you said you wanted, this is the whole point* — and it wouldn't manufacture.

What I wanted, it turned out, was to draw.

I want to be careful writing this because it's the kind of sentence that wants to be bigger than it is. I used to draw. In college and for a couple years after. Not seriously, not toward anything, just — I drew. I had a small case of pens and a notebook and I drew plants and corners of rooms and the occasional unflattering portrait of a roommate. I have not drawn, in any real way, in maybe five years. The pens are in a drawer in the spare room. I know exactly which drawer.

I sat with the coffee and the wanting was: get the pens.

The thing that almost stopped me was specific and stupid. I thought *you don't even know if those pens still work.* And underneath that, the bigger version: *if you go get them and they don't work, or if you do get them and what comes out is bad, then you've ruined the wanting.* The wanting was, at that moment, perfect, because it was unspent. Going to the drawer was a risk against the wanting itself.

I went to the drawer.

Most of the pens were dead. Two worked. There was a sketchbook with maybe ten pages of old drawings in it and a lot of empty pages after. I brought the two working pens and the sketchbook back to the couch and put them next to the coffee and looked at them for a while.

I drew the coffee cup. That was the first thing. Just the cup, from the angle I was looking at it, with the steam stopped and the handle turned slightly away. It took maybe fifteen minutes. It was not good. The handle was wrong and I had given the cup a strange lean it didn't actually have.

But while I was drawing it, something happened that I want to describe carefully because I don't want to oversell it. The tabs closed. Not all of them, not forever, but the surveillance operation I had running on Wednesday's lunch — the *am I being fun enough, is this landing, what does Mei think* — that whole apparatus went quiet, because there was no one to perform for and no outcome to manage. The cup didn't care if I drew it well. The pen didn't care. There was no one in the apartment who was going to see the drawing and form an opinion that I would then have to manage.

I drew the cup, and then I drew the corner of the window, and then I drew a plant we have on the sill, badly, and then I drew Sam's slipper which he had left by the couch. None of these were good. That is, I think, the thing I want to write down. *None of these were good and I did not care.* I cannot remember the last time I did something for an hour where the question of whether it was good did not enter the room.

At some point I noticed I was hungry and made toast and brought it back and kept drawing. I drew the toast. The toast was the worst one, weirdly flat, with crumbs that looked like punctuation. I laughed at it out loud, in the empty apartment, which is a thing I almost never do alone.

It was 11:40 when I looked at the time. Three hours and ten minutes had gone somewhere. They had not felt like three hours. They had not felt like any specific length. They had just been the time it took to draw a cup and a window and a plant and a slipper and a piece of toast.

Sam came home around 1. I had put the sketchbook away by then — I want to be honest, I put it away before he got back, I didn't want to explain it, I wanted to keep it for now — and I was reading actually, the book finally, for about the last twenty minutes. The book was fine. It was not what the morning had been about.

What I keep coming back to, sitting here Saturday night writing this, is that I had been wrong about what I wanted for weeks. I had been telling myself *book, quiet, couch* and the actual answer was *pens, table, no one watching.* The book was a stand-in. It was the socially legible version of the wanting. *I want to read* is a sentence other people understand. *I want to draw badly for three hours* is harder to say, and so apparently I had not even said it to myself.

I don't know what to do with this yet. I am not going to announce a thing. I am not going to put *drawing* on the calendar, which would kill it. The pens are back in the drawer. The sketchbook is on the shelf. They will be there next time Sam has the Saturday thing, or the next quiet hour I find.

The wanting was there. It had been there. I just hadn't been quiet long enough to hear it.

entries.prompt: What's a small thing someone did for you recently — a favor, a kindness, a bit of effort — that you haven't quite let yourself feel the weight of? Take me to the moment it happened and what you did with it.
entries.content:
Sunday night.

Tuesday last week, I think it was. I had been complaining — not really complaining, more like narrating tiredly — about the coffee at the office being bad. Not in a pointed way. Just in the way you say things when you're standing in a kitchen waiting for a kettle. Sam was making dinner and I was leaning on the counter and I said something like *the coffee there has gotten genuinely insulting, I don't know what they did to it.* He made a sympathetic noise. We moved on. I forgot I'd said it within about ninety seconds.

Thursday morning I went to grab my bag and there was a small thing in the side pocket I don't usually use. A travel-size bag of beans from the place we like, the one a few blocks from us, and a little hand grinder — the cheap kind, plastic and metal, maybe twenty dollars — and a folded note that said *for the office. love you.*

I stood there in the entryway holding it.

What I did with it, in that moment, was — I want to be accurate — I felt a quick warm thing and then almost immediately started doing math on it. *That's so sweet. He didn't have to do that. The grinder was probably fifteen or twenty dollars, the beans maybe twelve, so call it thirty-five, and the trip to the coffee place is fifteen minutes round trip, so —* I caught myself doing this and was a little horrified. I was pricing the gesture. As if knowing the cost would tell me what to do with it.

I took it to work. I used it Thursday and Friday. The coffee was much better. People at the kitchen at work asked me about the grinder and I explained it in a slightly offhand way — *oh, Sam picked this up for me, the office coffee has been rough* — like it was a piece of equipment rather than a thing my partner did because he had heard me.

That's the part I want to sit with. He had heard me. I had said one tired sentence on a Tuesday and he had filed it and acted on it two days later without telling me he was going to. Not as a big gesture. Not on a birthday. On a Wednesday, presumably, on his lunch break or after work, he had walked to the coffee place and then to wherever and bought a grinder and put them in my bag the night before so I would find them in the morning. The whole sequence required him to take a sentence I barely meant and decide it was worth doing something about.

I think I haven't let myself feel the weight of it because the weight is heavier than the object. The object is thirty-five dollars of coffee equipment. The weight is *someone is paying attention to you in a register you are not paying attention to yourself in.* I didn't know I wanted better coffee at work. I had registered it as a low background annoyance, the kind I don't even bring up because it isn't worth bringing up. He had registered it as something to fix.

There's a thing happening in me when I try to receive this fully. It's the same thing that happened with Mei's compliment, weirdly. A small fast argument starts up. *He just likes doing nice things, this is who he is, it's not specifically about you, he would have done it for anyone he lived with.* And then under that: *don't make this bigger than it is, it's a grinder, he picked up a grinder.* And then under *that*, which is the one I notice now and didn't on Thursday: *if you let this land all the way, then you have to be someone who is loved that attentively, and you have to walk around being loved like that, and that is — *

That is what. I don't have the end of the sentence. Something about it being a lot to carry. Something about how being loved attentively means being seen attentively, and being seen attentively is the thing I've spent eight entries describing as the scariest thing on earth when Mei does it. But it isn't scary when Sam does it. It's just — heavy. In the good way, I think. In the way a real coat is heavy.

I did thank him Thursday morning. I kissed him and said *this is so sweet, thank you,* and he said *of course, that coffee sounded terrible.* The exchange was about ten seconds. Adequate. Not wrong. But ten seconds is not the size of the thing, and I knew it wasn't, and I let it be ten seconds anyway, because letting it be longer would have required me to say something I didn't have ready. *You heard me when I didn't even know I was saying anything.* I can't say that to him at 7:45am while he's eating cereal. Or — I could. I just don't.

What I want to put down is that the gesture has been sitting on my desk at work all week, in the form of the grinder, and every morning I use it I have a small clean moment that is just — Sam thought of this. That's the whole moment. Sam thought of this on a Wednesday. The coffee tastes better and also someone I live with is paying attention to me.

I'm going to tell him tonight. Not a big speech. Just — *hey, the coffee thing meant a lot, more than I said Thursday.* One sentence. He'll probably say *oh, good, I'm glad,* and we'll move on. But the sentence will have been said. The ten seconds will have been twelve.

That's the small thing I can do with it. Not match the gesture. Not price it. Just let him know it landed where he hoped it would land. Which is, I think, all anyone giving a small kindness actually wants — to find out it arrived.

I'd like to be the kind of person who tells people things arrived.

I'm going to go tell him.

entries.prompt: When did you last get genuinely annoyed at someone this week — even just a flash of it that you didn't show? Take me to the moment, what they did or said, and what the version of you that didn't speak up was wanting to say back.
entries.content:
Monday night.

Today, around 3pm. Working session with Mei. The same kind of session as the one where she said the structural-part thing, except this one had a different shape.

We were going through a doc I had drafted over the weekend. Not on her time — on mine, on Sunday afternoon, after the drawing morning had ended and Sam was reading on the other end of the couch. I had spent maybe two and a half hours on it. It wasn't perfect but it was solid. I was, again, a little proud of it going in. I notice I keep being a little proud going in. I should learn something from how often that comes up.

She was reading through it on her screen, sharing, scrolling. I was watching the cursor move. And about halfway down the second page she said, "I think we should restructure this whole section. What if we led with the constraints instead of the objectives? It would make the rest of it land better."

And the version she described was — fine. It was a fine version. It wasn't better than what I had. It wasn't worse, either. It was a lateral move. The kind of restructure that takes two hours to execute and produces a doc that is approximately as good as the one you started with.

I said, "Yeah, that could work. Want me to take a pass at it tonight?"

What I wanted to say, what was right there behind my teeth, was: *that's not actually better, that's just different, and I spent Sunday on this and I don't want to spend tonight on it too because the version we have is fine and you know it's fine and I think you're restructuring it because restructuring it is the move you make in these sessions.*

That last part is the part I am embarrassed to have thought, and also the part I think is true.

Mei restructures things. That is, in some way, her contribution. She comes into a doc and she rearranges it. It is sometimes a real improvement and it is sometimes a lateral move dressed as an improvement, and I have been working with her long enough to know the difference, and today was a lateral move. And I said yes to it because saying *I don't think that's better* would have required me to defend my version against hers, in a register I have not built the muscle for, with a person whose good opinion I am — see entries one through nine — chronically managing.

The annoyance was a flash. Maybe three seconds. It went up my chest and into my jaw and I swallowed it and produced *yeah, that could work* in a voice that was, I think, completely neutral. Devon would not have caught it. Sam would have caught it. Mei did not, or if she did she gave no sign.

And then for the rest of the session I was annoyed underneath. Not at the restructure. At myself, mostly, for agreeing to it instantly. And at her, a little, for not noticing that what she had asked for was two more hours of my time on a doc that didn't need it. And at the dynamic, which is the bigger thing: that in our sessions she proposes and I execute, and the proposals are sometimes great and sometimes lateral, and I treat all of them as great because the cost of pushing back on a lateral one is higher than the cost of just doing the work.

Higher for me. Not for her. She would, I think, be totally fine if I said *I don't think the restructure adds enough to be worth the time, can we leave it.* She would probably say *okay, fair.* She is not, in my actual experience of her, a person who punishes disagreement. The person who would punish me for disagreeing with Mei is me.

I keep finding her in rooms I built.

What the un-spoken version of me wanted to say, written out cleanly, was: *I worked on this Sunday. I think the structure is good. The version you're describing is a sideways move and I'd rather spend the time pushing the back half forward than reorganizing the front. Can we leave the structure and look at section four together?*

That's a sentence. It is a real sentence I could have said. It is direct without being cold. It would have shifted the session toward the part that actually needed work. It would have, possibly, made the session better for both of us.

I did not say it. I said *yeah, that could work.* And I will, tonight, after I finish this entry, spend an hour or two restructuring a doc I do not think needs restructuring, and I will send it to her tomorrow morning, and she will say *much better,* and it will not actually be much better but the loop will close and I will have paid the tax.

I want to write down what I notice about the annoyance itself, because I think the annoyance is information I usually throw away. The annoyance was the part of me that knew. The annoyance saw the lateral move and named it before my mouth could spackle over it. The annoyance is, in some sense, the most accurate observer in the room. And I treat it as a problem to manage rather than a signal to read.

If I had paused on the annoyance for five seconds — the same five seconds I keep promising myself with Devon and the yeses — I might have heard *this is a lateral move, you don't have to do it.* The annoyance was telling me that. It had the data. I shut it down because annoyance-at-Mei is unsafe, and so I lost the data with it.

I am going to do the restructure tonight. I am not going to make this entry the one where I refuse, because I'm not going to refuse, and I'd rather not lie at the kitchen table.

But I want to put down, for the next time: the flash is information. The flash knew before I did. The job is not to suppress the flash — the job is to read it before swallowing it. Even if I still swallow.

Even five seconds. Just to read it first.

I keep thinking I'll try.

entries.prompt: Think about something you've quietly decided about yourself lately — a verdict, a label, a conclusion — that you've been treating as fact. What's the evidence you've been using, and where did it come from?
entries.content:
Tuesday night.

The verdict I've been treating as fact is: *I am a person who can't actually push back on Mei.*

I've been carrying this around like it's a feature of the landscape. Not a question, not a working theory — a fact about how I am built. As fixed as being right-handed.

I want to look at the evidence, because last night I wrote the entry about the restructure and then did the restructure, and somewhere between sending the new version to Mei this morning and watching her reply *much better!* with an exclamation point I didn't quite believe, I noticed I was filing yesterday into the same drawer I file everything else. *See, you couldn't say it. You can't say it. This is who you are with her.* Another data point for the verdict. Case closed, again.

So what's the evidence.

The doc thing yesterday. The chart from a few weeks back, where I said *good catch* and went home and spiraled instead of saying anything real. The compliment about the structural part, where I argued with her in my head while thanking her with my mouth. A handful of working sessions over the last six months where she's proposed something and I've agreed and gone off to execute. The general shape of how our sessions go. That's the file.

Here's what I notice when I actually look at it. Every item in the file is a moment where I *didn't* push back. The verdict — *I can't* — is built entirely on instances of not doing it. Which is a strange way to prove a capacity. It would be like concluding I can't swim because I haven't been in a pool. The not-doing is real. The can't is an inference I've laid on top.

And when I try to find counter-evidence — moments where I did push back, on Mei or on anyone like her — I have to think harder, but they exist. The K thing in the standup, two months ago, where I raised the timing issue. That was pushing back, on someone whose work I had no special standing to question, in front of a group that included Mei. I did that. I did it scared but I did it. There's also a thing from probably six months ago where Mei wanted to move a deadline and I said I didn't think the team could absorb it, and she said okay and we kept the original date. I had completely forgotten that until just now. *Forgotten.* A piece of evidence directly contradicting the verdict, and I had filed it under nothing.

That's the part that stops me. The verdict has been curating its own evidence. It keeps the doc from yesterday and discards the deadline from June. It keeps the chart and discards the K thing. The file looks airtight because the file has been edited.

Where did the verdict come from. I think — and this is me guessing — it came from somewhere much earlier than Mei. Mei is the current host. She is not the origin. The shape of *there is a person whose good opinion I cannot afford to risk, and so I make myself smaller in front of them* — that shape is older than this job. I can find versions of it going back to college, to a specific professor I would not disagree with even when I knew I was right. To high school, to a friend group where having a wrong opinion was expensive. The verdict didn't form when I met Mei. The verdict was already formed and Mei walked into the slot.

Which means the evidence I've been using isn't actually evidence about Mei at all. It's evidence about a pattern I bring to certain people, the ones who occupy that slot. Mei is not the cause. She's the current location.

This matters because if the verdict is *I can't push back on Mei,* the only solutions are about Mei — manage her, avoid her, find a different working partner, hope she changes. But if the verdict is *I have a long-running pattern of going small in front of people whose approval feels load-bearing,* then Mei is one instance and the pattern is the thing. And patterns are — I don't want to use the wrong word here — patterns are, at least, things you can see operating. You can watch them happen. Whereas a fixed fact about yourself you just have to live around.

The other piece of evidence I've been treating as fact is harder to write because it's vaguer. It's something like *I am a person who feels things more intensely on the inside than the situation warrants.* The chart, the lunch, the compliment, the grinder — every one of those entries has me reporting an outsized interior reaction to a normal-sized exterior event, and at some point I started concluding from this that I am, constitutively, too much on the inside. Too loud. Too reactive. A person whose interior is miscalibrated.

But I notice I have no way to check this. I cannot see anyone else's interior. I am comparing my full interior weather to other people's outsides. Of course mine looks louder. I'm the only one I can hear from the inside. Devon could be running the same surveillance operation I run at lunch and I would have no way to know. Mei could have her own version of the chart-spiral and I would only see the thumbs up.

So that verdict — *my interior is too much* — is built on a comparison that is structurally unfair. I don't actually have the data to know if my interior is louder than average, quieter than average, or exactly average. I just have the data that mine is loud to me. Which it would be, by definition, because it's mine and I'm in it.

I'm not going to pretend I've dismantled either verdict by writing this. They will both be back tomorrow. The *can't push back on Mei* one will be back probably by Thursday's session. The *too much on the inside* one will be back the next time I spiral on something small.

But I want to put down, for when they come back: the evidence is curated. The file has been edited. There is a deadline in June I forgot. There is a standup in August I had to push to remember. The verdict is keeping the receipts that confirm it and losing the ones that don't.

The verdict is not neutral. It has preferences.

That's the thing I want to remember. Not that the verdicts are wrong — I don't know if they're wrong. Just that they are not, as I have been treating them, simply observations. They are arguments. And arguments can be examined.

I keep thinking I'll examine them.

Tonight, at least, I did.

entries.prompt: Where did you feel most like yourself this week — even just for a stretch of an hour or a few minutes? Take me into it: what you were doing, who was or wasn't around, and what was different about the inside of your head right then.
entries.content:
Wednesday night.

The stretch was Tuesday morning, before work. About forty minutes, between 6:50 and 7:30. I had woken up earlier than I needed to — that thing where your eyes open at 6:40 and you know going back to sleep won't take — and instead of doing what I usually do, which is reach for the phone and burn the unexpected time on nothing, I got up.

It was still mostly dark. Sam was asleep. The apartment had that pre-anyone quality, the air not yet stirred up by a day. I made coffee — with the grinder, which I had brought back home over the weekend because I wanted to use it on a Tuesday morning and not just at the office — and the smell filled the kitchen in a way it doesn't when I'm rushing. I stood there while the water heated and just watched the window get a little less black.

I didn't have a plan. That's the part I want to get right. I wasn't doing a thing. I was just up, with coffee, in a quiet apartment.

I ended up at the kitchen table with the sketchbook. I had been thinking about it on and off since Saturday and I think some part of me had been waiting for the next chance, even though I had told myself I wasn't going to put it on a calendar. Apparently the body finds its own calendar. I drew the salt and pepper shakers, which were sitting where Sam had left them after dinner. They are nothing — a small white ceramic pair, slightly chipped on one of them. I drew them for maybe twenty minutes. I got the chip wrong, in a way that almost made me laugh, and kept going.

What was different about the inside of my head right then.

The surveillance was off. That's the cleanest way I can say it. The tabs that are usually open at 8am — what's on the calendar, what did I not finish yesterday, what mood will Mei be in, what's the first message I'm going to get on Slack — none of those were running. Or if they were, they were so quiet I couldn't hear them. The room my head was in was a small one, with the salt shaker in it and the coffee and the pen.

It was not, I want to be honest, a transcendent thing. I was not having some kind of experience. I was sitting at a table drawing badly. But the inside of my head matched the outside of the situation in a way it almost never does. The eight-year-old at the board was not in the room. The person managing Mei's opinion was not in the room. The version of me that is constantly making sure she's being a good enough lunch companion was not in the room. There was just a person and a salt shaker and the pen wasn't moving particularly well.

I think the key feature was that nothing was being produced for anyone. The drawing wasn't going to be shown. The morning wasn't going to be reported on. There was no audience, including the imaginary one I usually carry around. The drawing was going to exist on a page that would close into a book that would go on a shelf, and that was the whole arc.

When I think about it next to lunch with Devon and Mei, the difference is almost embarrassing in its simplicity. At lunch I am performing, partly, even when I don't want to be, because there are people there who will form impressions. At the kitchen table at 7am there is no one to form an impression and so there is no impression to manage and so the part of me that manages impressions has nothing to do and goes quiet. It is not that I become a different person at the kitchen table. It is that the apparatus that runs constantly when other people are around finally has nothing to react to and stops running.

Which is sort of a relief and sort of a depressing thought. The relief is — there is a version of me that exists when the apparatus is off. She draws salt shakers. She is fine. She is, actually, kind of pleasant company, in that she is not anxious or performing or grading. The depressing part is that this version seems to require a very specific set of conditions to appear: alone, no agenda, nothing being made for anyone.

But I don't know if that's actually true or if I just haven't found her in other configurations yet. The four seconds at lunch, the kitchen at the office with the kettle, the drawing morning Saturday, now this. Those are four moments in maybe a month. They have a shape. They are all moments where I stopped producing. They are not all moments where I was alone — the four seconds at lunch had two people right next to me. So it isn't really aloneness that does it. It's the production stopping.

Sam came into the kitchen around 7:30. He saw the sketchbook, didn't make a thing of it, just said *oh, hey,* and got coffee, and I closed the book and moved it to the side, not because I was hiding it from him exactly but because the morning was already shifting and I wanted to let it. He sat down across from me and we didn't talk much. He was reading something on his phone. I drank the rest of my coffee.

That last fifteen minutes, with him there and not talking, was almost the same as the forty before it. The apparatus stayed mostly off. He doesn't, it turns out, switch it on. I had assumed any other person in the room would, but Sam doesn't, or doesn't have to. That's worth knowing.

I want to put this down because I keep thinking the question is *how do I become less performed at work* and maybe the more useful question for now is *where are the places the apparatus is already off, and can I find them more often.* Tuesday morning is one. Saturday with the pens was one. The kitchen at the office was a small one. I don't have to engineer a different version of myself at lunch with Mei. I can just notice where the version I want already exists and go there more.

Forty minutes before work. That's not a calendar item. But it's a thing I could do again Thursday, if I wake up early. I'm not going to set an alarm for it. I'm just going to know that if my eyes open at 6:40, there's something to do with the time.

The salt shaker is on the page. The chip is wrong. I like it.

entries.prompt: Some week this has been — Mei, the restructure, the verdict, then the quiet Tuesday morning that felt like yours. Pull back from all of that for a minute: who in your life have you barely thought about lately, and when their name surfaces now, what comes with it?
entries.content:
Wednesday night, still.

The name that surfaces is my brother.

I have a brother. I have not written him into any of these entries, which is itself a thing. He is two years older. He lives a six-hour drive from our parents and a flight from me. He has two kids now, the younger one almost two, and I have met that one exactly once, when she was four months old and slept through my visit.

When I think about him I notice first that I cannot tell you what he is currently working on. I know the general shape — something steady, something in a field I don't fully follow — but if someone asked me at a party *what does your brother do,* I would have to do a small fast reconstruction and the answer would be vague. That is a thing about us. Not estrangement. Not a fight. Just — drift, of a particular kind, where neither person did anything wrong and the distance grew anyway.

We text on birthdays. We text in the family group chat when Mom sends a picture of something. He sent me a video maybe three months ago of his older kid doing something with a hose, and I sent back the laughing emoji and a *she's getting so big* and that was the exchange. I have not called him in — I had to think about this — probably eight months. Maybe more. He has not called me either. Neither of us is the bad guy in this. We are both, apparently, fine with eight months.

What comes with the name when it surfaces.

The first thing is a small specific memory I haven't thought about in years. We are maybe twelve and fourteen. Our parents are out and we are supposed to be doing homework and instead we are watching some movie we are not supposed to watch on the living room TV, ready to switch the channel if we hear the car. He is on the couch and I am on the floor and at some point he slides down to the floor too because the angle is better, and we sit there shoulder to shoulder for an hour and a half not really talking, just watching, and when the movie ends he says *that was so stupid* and I say *yeah,* and we both mean *that was great.*

I don't know why that one comes up. We had thousands of evenings. That one is the one in the drawer.

The second thing that comes with the name is a low guilt. Not sharp. Not the chart-error kind. More like a debt I have stopped checking the balance on. Every time I think about calling him I think *it has been so long, the call would be huge,* and then the call is too big to make on a Tuesday on the couch — the same exact mechanism as Riley, I notice, the same shape, the gap getting bigger because I haven't closed it, the closing getting harder because the gap is bigger. I am running this pattern with at least two people. Probably more. This is, apparently, a thing I do.

The third thing — and I want to be careful with this one because I'm not sure it's fair — is a small comparison. He has the kids and the steady thing and the house and all of it. He looks, from the outside, like someone whose life has settled into its shape. I do not think he is, internally, less of a mess than I am. He's my brother. I have seen him at three in the morning crying about a girl in 2009. I know he has weather. But the *outside* of his life looks finished in a way that mine doesn't, and when his name surfaces there's a small flick of *he figured it out and you are still figuring it out.* I notice the flick. I don't fully buy it. But it's there.

What surprises me, sitting with the name for a minute longer, is how much warmth is underneath all of this. The drift has not actually changed how I feel about him. If he called me right now I would be, instantly, glad to hear his voice. Not awkward. Glad. The eight months would not be in the call. We would just talk. We have always been able to do that. The drift is a thing about logistics and inertia, not about whether I love him. I do. I just haven't done anything about it for a long time.

There's a sentence forming that I want to write down before I lose it. *I have let some of my closest people become people I think about sometimes.* Riley. My brother. Probably one or two others if I sat with it. I have a category in my life now called *people I love who I am not actively in touch with,* and the category has been growing, quietly, while I have been busy managing how I look to people I see every day. I am spending the bulk of my attention on Mei's three-word Slack messages and almost none on the brother who sat on the floor with me for an hour and a half and said *that was so stupid.*

That is not a verdict. That is just what I notice when I look at where the attention has gone.

I am not going to call him tonight. I want to but I also know the *I'll do it tomorrow when I can properly* sentence is loading itself, and I'd rather do the smaller true thing than the larger fake one. The smaller true thing is — text him. Not about anything. Just *hey, thinking about you, how's the small one walking these days.* Ninety seconds. The same shape as the Riley text last week, which actually got answered, which actually moved something.

I'm going to do that before I close the laptop.

His name is in my head and the warmth is there and the apparatus, for once, is not telling me to make it bigger than it needs to be. Just text. Just the sentence. Let him know I'm thinking about him. Find out it arrived.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been good at for so long that you've stopped giving yourself any credit for it? Take me to a recent moment where you did the thing almost on autopilot — what was happening, and what would it have looked like to someone watching who didn't know you?
entries.content:
Thursday night.

I texted my brother. He wrote back within an hour with a video of the small one walking in a way that is barely walking — more like falling forward at high speed and catching herself with each foot. He wrote *she is unstoppable and also very stoppable.* I laughed out loud at my desk. So that happened. Filing it.

The prompt. I had to sit with this one because the obvious answers felt either too small or too vain to write down. *I'm good at structure.* Mei's already said that and I've spent an entry arguing with it. *I'm good at making coffee.* Sure, but come on.

What I landed on is: I am extremely good at running a meeting.

Not leading one, exactly. Running one. The thing where there are six people on a call and an agenda that's slightly wrong and one person who hasn't spoken in fifteen minutes and another person who has spoken too much, and the clock is moving, and somebody has to make sure the actual decisions happen and the actual people get heard and the meeting ends on time with everyone clear on what's next. That thing. I am good at that thing. I have been good at it for years. I do not give myself any credit for it because it happens in the texture of my day so constantly that I have stopped seeing it as a skill at all.

The recent moment. This morning. A check-in on a project that has been quietly sliding — not anyone's fault, just one of those projects where the original scope has crept and nobody has wanted to be the one to say so. Six people, forty-five minutes. I wasn't the most senior person in the room. I wasn't even technically the meeting owner. I had just been the one who put the agenda together because no one else had.

What I did, on autopilot, in the first five minutes: opened with a one-sentence framing of why we were there, in a way that didn't blame anyone for the slide. Asked the quietest person on the call her view first, before the loud people had set the temperature. Caught a comment from one of the senior people that was technically off-topic and parked it for later in a way that didn't make him feel parked. When two people started to talk over each other, I said *go ahead, J,* and J went ahead, and the other person waited, and neither of them registered that I had just decided who got the floor.

What I did at minute thirty: noticed we were drifting on a question that wasn't going to get resolved in this meeting and said *I think we need to take this offline — can K and I sync tomorrow and bring back a recommendation?* K said yes. The room exhaled. We moved on.

What I did at minute forty-two: said *okay, three minutes left, let me try to summarize where we landed,* and gave a summary that was eighty percent accurate and twenty percent slightly more decided than we actually were, which is the trick — you summarize forward, just a little, so that disagreeing with the summary becomes the thing that makes the decision. Nobody disagreed. The summary became the decision. We ended on time.

What it would have looked like to someone watching who didn't know me.

I think it would have looked like nothing. That is the actual answer. It would have looked like a meeting that went fine. The watcher would not have noticed the quiet person being asked first, because the quiet person spoke and so it didn't seem like a thing. They would not have noticed the parked comment, because the senior person didn't feel parked. They would not have noticed the summary doing the deciding, because the summary felt like a recap. The whole thing would have looked like a normal meeting that happened to go well, and the watcher would have concluded *oh, the team works well together,* which is the conclusion you reach when someone is doing the work invisibly.

Which is the conclusion I reach about myself.

I want to write that down because I think it's the actual finding. The reason I don't give myself credit for this is that the skill is, by design, invisible. Its quality is measured by how little the meeting felt like it was being run. If anyone in the room had thought *Maya is running this meeting,* I would have been doing it worse. The whole craft is to be the one steering without the steering being visible. And then I, watching myself from the inside, conclude that nothing happened, because nothing visible happened. The skill erases its own evidence.

I have been doing this for probably eight years. Across a few jobs. Across hundreds — thousands? — of meetings. The muscle is enormous and I treat it like air.

What I notice is that this is structurally identical to the verdict thing from Tuesday. The evidence of competence is being curated out of the file the same way the evidence of pushback was. The chart error stays. The thousand meetings that ran cleanly disappear, because they ran cleanly, which made them invisible, which made them not count.

I think if Devon had run this morning's meeting the way I ran it, I would have walked away thinking *Devon is so good at that.* I would have specifically named it. I would have maybe even mentioned it to him later — *that was a really well-run room.* I extend that to him without trying. I cannot extend it to myself, again, still.

But here is the thing I want to put down. The meeting today was good. Not because of luck and not because the team works well together. Because I ran it well.
