Sam and I had already passed them and were two aisles over by the time it hit me. We were standing in front of the yogurts and Sam was reading a label and I was looking at nothing in particular and then I just heard it again in my head — *this pear is unacceptable* — and a laugh came out of me that was not a normal laugh. It was the kind that comes from somewhere lower, that you cannot stage, that bends you forward a little. I made a sound I do not usually make. Sam looked over.

I tried to say *the kid* and I could not get through *the kid* without laughing again, harder, and then Sam started laughing because I was laughing, even though he had not heard the kid, and then I managed *the pear* and *unacceptable* in some order and Sam fully lost it, the way he does maybe twice a year, the laugh where his face goes red. We were standing in front of the yogurts shaking. A woman pushed her cart past us with the careful neutral expression people use to give other people privacy in public.

It went on for maybe two minutes. We would calm down and then one of us would say *unacceptable* in the kid's voice and we would start again. By the end my stomach hurt. Actually hurt. I had not laughed to the point of stomach pain in — I genuinely cannot remember. A long time. Long enough that the soreness afterward was a kind of news from a body part I had not heard from in that register in maybe a year.

What it felt like coming out.

I want to try to describe this because I think it is the actual thing. The laugh did not feel like an event I produced. It felt like an event that happened to me. The autopilot — the one that runs lunch with Devon and Mei, the one that produces correct laughs at correct moments — was completely offline. The autopilot does not have access to this register. The autopilot does the social laugh, the *that's funny* laugh, the *I am with you in this story* laugh. It does not do the *bent forward in a grocery aisle making a sound I do not make* laugh. That laugh comes from somewhere the apparatus cannot reach.

It felt like being caught. Not caught in the bad sense. Caught the way a fish is caught — pulled from one element into another, briefly, against my will, by something that had nothing to do with me. The kid had nothing to do with me. The pear had nothing to do with me. Sam laughing with me had something to do with me but only after the fact. The thing that had set it off was an absurd small moment in a grocery store and I had been, apparently, available enough to it that it could land, and when it landed it produced a laugh that was bigger than the moment, because the laugh was not just for the moment — the laugh was for whatever I had been holding that needed somewhere to go.

That is the part I want to write down. The laugh was disproportionate to the kid. The kid was funny but not stomach-pain funny. The stomach pain was an interest payment on something else. On — I do not know exactly. On weeks of carrying things carefully. On entries about bracing and spackling and the eight-year-old at the board. On Mei in the hallway and the voice in the bathroom and the bench I have not gone to. On being thirty-one and accumulating, in some quiet ledger, a backlog of unspent feeling that the kid with the pear, somehow, gave a clean exit.

I noticed afterward that I felt loose. Physically. My shoulders were lower. We finished the shopping in this slightly altered state, where any small thing was funny — the way a pile of avocados had been stacked, the song that came on, the man at the fish counter who looked exactly like an actor neither of us could name. We were in the silly register for the rest of the trip. It lasted maybe forty minutes total. By the time we got home it had passed and I was a regular person again, putting groceries away.

But I want to put down that the laugh was, possibly, the most un-performed thing I have done in front of another person in a long time. More un-performed than the conversation with Sam Sunday night, which was real but careful. The laugh was not careful. The laugh did not check whether it was landing. The laugh did not monitor Sam's face for whether he was laughing too. It just came out, and Sam's laugh came out, and the two laughs met in front of the yogurts without either of us having arranged the meeting.

I would like more of that. I do not know how to engineer it — I think the whole point is that you cannot engineer it, you can only be loose enough that when the kid with the pear shows up, you are available. But I want to remember that the laugh exists. That my body still has access to it. That the apparatus, which I have spent so many entries describing as the thing that runs the show, does not actually run the whole show. There is a layer underneath the apparatus that the apparatus cannot touch, and the kid at the grocery store reached it, and I made a sound I do not usually make, and Sam was there.

*This pear is unacceptable.*

I am still smiling about it.

entries.prompt: When did you last feel genuinely competent at something this week — not praised for it, just quietly good at it while you were doing it? Take me into the stretch of time and what your hands and head were actually doing.
entries.content:
Wednesday night.

Yesterday afternoon, between about 2 and 4. I was deep in the back half of the project — the part I have been protecting from Devon's deck and Mei's restructures and everything else that wants my Thursdays. The part that is just mine. There was a section that had been refusing to come together for about a week, where the logic kept almost working and then not, and I had been picking at it in twenty-minute increments between other things and getting nowhere.

Yesterday I had a clear two-hour block on the calendar. I closed Slack. I put the phone face down. I opened the doc and the supporting materials and a blank page next to them, and I went in.

What my hands were doing: typing, mostly, but not in the producing-a-thing way. More like sketching. I would write a version of a paragraph, decide it was wrong, delete it, write another. I was not precious about the deleting. I was not precious about the writing either. The two were the same motion. My hands were moving the way they move when I am drawing badly — the cup, the salt shaker — except in sentences. The pen does not care if the line is right. You just do another line.

What my head was doing is harder to describe because it was not, in the usual sense, thinking. It was holding the shape of the problem and rotating it. There is a thing that happens when I have been with a piece of work long enough that I stop having to consciously remember the constraints — they are just there, in the room, like furniture. I can move around them without bumping into them. Yesterday at 2pm the constraints clicked into furniture. By 2:20 I had stopped actively remembering what we had decided in the kickoff and what the dependency on the other team needed and what Mei had flagged in the last session. All of it was just — in the air around the doc.

And once that happened I could see the section. Really see it. The reason it had not been coming together was that I had been trying to make one paragraph do two jobs. Once I separated the jobs, the paragraph that had been refusing to close split into two paragraphs that closed in about fifteen minutes each. Thirty minutes of work, after a week of getting nowhere, because I had finally been quiet enough to see that the structure was wrong, not the wording.

I want to write down what it felt like from the inside while it was happening, because I think this is the prompt.

It felt like nothing. That is the most accurate thing I can say. There was no charge. No private *yes,* no satisfaction, no pride. There was just the doc, and my hands, and a slight forward lean, and the occasional small adjustment when something I had written ten minutes ago needed to come back and become something else. The room was quiet. My head was quiet. The apparatus that runs lunch and meetings and Slack was somewhere else entirely — not switched off exactly, but in a different building.

I noticed at one point — maybe 3:15 — that I had not looked at the time in over an hour. The two-hour block, which I had been protective of, which I had been treating as scarce, had been swallowed into the work and I had not been counting it. The counting is a thing I do most of the time, even when I do not realize. *How long have I been at this. How much is left. Am I making progress at the right rate.* All of that machinery had gone quiet, and the work was just happening at whatever speed the work happens at, which turned out to be the right speed.

This is, I think, the difference between competent and *quietly competent* and whatever the third thing is that I do not have a word for. Competent is a description. *Quietly competent* is a description with a wince in it. The third thing — what was happening yesterday afternoon — is a state, not a description. It is the state of being so inside the thing that there is no observer left to describe it. The describing comes after, like now, at the kitchen table on Wednesday night, twenty-eight hours later, trying to reconstruct it.

What I want to write down is that the work was good. Not good-for-me. Not good-given-the-constraints. Just — the section is good now. It does what it needs to do. When I read it back this morning I had a small moment of *wait, did I write this,* which was actually a kind of compliment, because the section read like it had always been the section, with no fingerprints of the week of struggle on it. The struggle had erased itself, the way the steering erases itself in a meeting. The two hours yesterday produced a section that does not show that the two hours happened.

The voice from yesterday's bathroom would say something about this. Would say *the section is fine, anyone could have written it, this is your job, you do not get a parade for doing your job.* I can hear it forming. I am going to head it off.

The section is good. The two hours were good. I was, while I was in them, the version of myself I was on Tuesday morning at 7am with the salt shaker, except deployed into work instead of into a drawing. The apparatus was off. The producing-for-an-audience layer was off. There was just me and the problem and a kind of patient turning of it until the shape became visible.

I want to know that this is available at work. Not just at the kitchen table at dawn, not just on the bench I have not been to. At the desk. In a two-hour block. With the doc open. The state can come there too.

That is the thing I am putting down. Not that I am good at my job — I have other entries for that. That there is a way of being at my job where the thing I have been finding in the eight-minute pockets is also reachable. The forty minutes Tuesday morning, the long way home, the drawing — and also yesterday, 2 to 4, in the doc, with no one watching.

The section is closed. The page is saved. My hands knew what they were doing, and so did my head, and for two hours they were the same hands and head.

That happened. I did that.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been carrying an opinion about lately — about a person, a project, a piece of news — that you haven't said out loud to anyone? Take me to the last time it crossed your mind and what kept it from leaving your head.
entries.content:
Thursday night.

The opinion is about Devon. Specifically: I think Devon is going to leave.

I have not said this out loud to anyone. I have barely said it to myself. It has been forming for maybe three weeks, in the way these things form — small data points landing in the same drawer until the drawer is heavy enough to notice.

The data points, if I lay them out. He has been on his phone more in meetings, in the specific way people are on their phones when they are texting someone who matters more than what is being discussed. He took two days off two weeks ago that he did not explain, which is not in itself a thing — Devon takes days off — except that when I asked him casually about it on Slack he gave a non-answer with a deflection emoji, which is not how Devon answers. He has stopped pushing back in the working sessions where he used to push back. The energy he brought to disagreeing with Mei has gone somewhere else. He is still pleasant. He is still funny. But the engagement has thinned, the way milk thins when you add water — the color almost the same, the substance different.

And on Tuesday at the coffee machine he said, completely in passing, *do you ever think about whether you would still be doing this in three years,* and then before I could answer he laughed and said *ignore me, I am being a person on a Tuesday,* and changed the subject.

People who are staying do not ask that question. People who are leaving ask that question and then pretend they didn't.

It crossed my mind most recently this afternoon, in the kitchen at the office. He came in for coffee while I was making tea and we did the small thing we do — *how's it going, fine, you, fine* — and I watched him pour and felt the opinion sitting in my chest like a small stone. *You are going to leave. I think within four months.* I almost said something. Not the opinion itself. Some adjacent thing — *are you doing okay,* or *anything you want to talk about.* But I did not. I said *did you see the email about the offsite,* and we talked about the offsite for ninety seconds and then he left.

What kept it from leaving my head.

A few things, layered. The first is that I am not sure I am right. The data points are real but they are also the kind of data points that could mean other things — he could be tired, he could be having a hard time at home, he could just be in a phase. The opinion is a pattern-match, and I have been wrong about pattern-matches before. Saying it out loud would commit me to a read I cannot fully back up, and if I am wrong it would be slightly mortifying, and Devon would know, in some small way, that I had been observing him in a register he did not consent to be observed in.

The second is that saying it would change the shape of how I show up with him. Right now I am still treating him as a peer who is staying. If I let myself think *Devon is leaving,* even in a sentence to one other person, then he becomes, in my head, someone on the way out, and I would start, without meaning to, to pre-grieve the loss of him. I would invest less in the small lunch easiness because the small lunch easiness has an end date. I do not want to do that yet. I would rather be wrong and surprised than right and already gone.

The third thing — and this is the part I notice with a kind of wince — is that the opinion has a private use to me that I have not fully admitted. If Devon leaves, the configuration at work shifts. The lunch with three becomes lunch with two, which becomes Mei and me, which is a different room. The buffer Devon provides — the easiness, the third energy that takes the pressure off the Mei-and-me dynamic — goes. And underneath the worry about losing him is a smaller, less generous thread of *what would my work life be without him as the easy one,* which is a question about me, not about him. I have been holding the opinion partly because looking at it requires me to look at how much I rely on Devon to make work feel survivable. That is not a thing I want to find out about myself by writing it in a notebook on a Thursday night, but here we are.

Who would I tell. Sam, probably, if I told anyone. He would receive it in the flat way he receives work things, which is fine — flat is correct for an unconfirmed read on a coworker. He would say *huh, what makes you think that,* and I would lay out the data points, and he would say *yeah, could be,* and the opinion would have a witness, which is what opinions need to stop circling. Right now it is circling because it is unwitnessed. It cannot leave my head because there is nowhere for it to go.

I am not going to tell Mei. Obviously. The opinion in her hands becomes a thing that requires action, and it is not ready to be action.

I might tell Sam tonight. Not as a Thing. Just — *I think Devon might be on his way out.* One sentence. See what he does with it.

The pear-laugh is still in my body somewhere from Saturday. The door-not-locked sentence from Sunday. A small accumulation of things I have started letting Sam see. This would be another one. Smaller than the Mei stuff, but the same shape — handing him the actual data instead of the surface.

The opinion does not have to be right to be sayable. That is, I think, the thing I keep forgetting. I have been treating *I might be wrong* as a reason to stay quiet, when it is actually just a feature of having opinions about other people's interiors. Of course I might be wrong. I am working from coffee-machine moments. The point of saying it is not to be right. The point is to stop carrying it alone.

I will tell him after this. One sentence. Devon. See.

entries.prompt: When was the last time you changed your mind about something — not a big philosophical shift, just a small revision where you'd been sure of one thing and then quietly weren't anymore? Take me to what you'd been thinking before, and what nudged it.
entries.content:
Friday night.

The thing I changed my mind about is the bench.

Not whether to go. I have not gone. The bench is still there, unsat-on, the pond presumably still has its ducks. What I changed my mind about is what the bench is for, or maybe more accurately, what was keeping me from it.

I had been telling myself, for months, that the obstacle was time. The fifteen extra minutes. The open-ended *until I was done* container that my week did not have a slot for. I wrote a whole entry about this last week. I was pretty pleased with the entry. The entry concluded — and I am paraphrasing my own paraphrasing — that the bench was a category of unconverted time I had not given myself permission to have, and the obstacle was a kind of embarrassment about needing it.

I believed that when I wrote it. I still partly believe it. But something happened Wednesday that revised it.

Wednesday I had, for reasons that do not matter, an unexpected forty-five minutes between a meeting that ended early and a thing I needed to be home for. I was already out of the office. I was, in fact, three blocks from the park. The weather was fine. I had no errands. I had nothing in my hands. The exact configuration of conditions I have been telling myself I needed in order to go to the bench had assembled itself, unbidden, in the middle of a Wednesday afternoon, and dropped me at the entrance.

I did not go.

I walked the other direction, toward home, and got there twenty-five minutes early, and sat on the couch, and looked at my phone.

That is what I want to write about, because the bench-entry version of me — the one from a week ago — would have predicted that given those conditions, I would go. The whole framing was *I cannot find the time.* The time found me. And I declined it. Which means the framing was wrong, or at least incomplete, and I have been turning over what the actual obstacle is.

What I had been thinking before: that the bench was a thing I wanted, blocked by structural constraints — time, embarrassment, the calendar logic. That if the constraints lifted, I would arrive. The wanting was the constant; the not-going was the variable. Solve the variable and the wanting expresses itself.

What I am quietly not sure of anymore: that the wanting is, in the way I had been describing it, even there.

Let me try to say this carefully because I am not sure I have it yet. There is *a* wanting around the bench. I can feel it. When I think about the bench I get the small bright thing in my chest that tells me the bench is in the right category of imagined activity. But Wednesday afternoon, standing three blocks away with forty-five minutes in my hand, what I noticed was that the wanting did not — and I am trying to find the right word — did not *activate.* It stayed an idea. It did not become a leg moving in the direction of the park. The idea of the bench is alive. The motion toward the bench is, apparently, not.

This is different from the obstacle being time or embarrassment. This is the obstacle being something more like — I have been enjoying wanting the bench more than I would enjoy the bench. The wanting has been doing work for me. It has been a marker of the kind of person I would like to be. *Someone who sits on benches.* Someone with that category of time. As long as the bench is unsat-on, I get to be the person who is *almost* that, who is heading toward it, who is recognizing the lack and intending to address it. If I sat on the bench, the wanting would discharge, and I would be back to whatever I am the rest of the time, which is a person who looked at her phone on the couch for twenty-five minutes when she could have been at the pond.

I do not love this revision. The earlier version was more flattering. It cast me as someone with a real desire blocked by external friction. The new version is less generous: I have been carrying the desire as a kind of decoration, and the friction has been partly self-generated to keep the decoration in place. The wanting has been the point, not the bench.

What nudged it was specifically Wednesday, but also the drawing. The drawing was a thing I genuinely wanted and the wanting expressed itself the moment the conditions allowed. Sat on the couch with coffee, no one watching, and the wanting moved my legs to the drawer. No friction. No *I have not earned this.* The body just went. I have been comparing the bench to the drawing and assuming they were the same kind of wanting, and Wednesday made me notice they are not. The drawing is real. The bench, as currently configured in my head, is more like — an aspirational accessory.

This does not mean I do not want to sit on a bench. It means the version I have been wanting is partly imaginary. The actual bench, on an actual Wednesday, with actual time, did not pull me. Some other version — bench-as-symbol, bench-as-self-image — has been pulling me, and that version cannot be satisfied by sitting, because sitting would end it.

The small revision is just this: not every wanting in my head is a real one in the sense that it would survive contact with its object. Some are, like the drawing. Some are not, like the bench. And I have been treating all of them the same, and then being surprised when some of them do not move me when given the chance.

The honest move, I think, is to either go to the bench and find out — actually go, and find out whether the activation comes when I am there even if it does not come on the way — or admit the bench is a placeholder for something else and stop using it as a measure of my own progress.

I do not have to decide tonight. The pond is not asking.

But I noticed I had been sure, and now I am not, and that is the small thing.

entries.prompt: Think of something you've been doing the same way for years — a route, a routine, an order of operations — that you've never really questioned. Walk me through the last time you did it, and what would actually be at stake in changing it.
entries.content:
Saturday night.

The thing I have been doing the same way for years is the order of my mornings on workdays. Specifically: phone before anything else.

It goes like this. Alarm at 6:50. Reach for the phone, which is on the nightstand, charging. Turn off the alarm. Stay in bed and check, in order — and I had to actually pay attention this morning to confirm the order, because I have done it so many times I had stopped noticing — email first, then Slack, then the news app, then one social thing, then back to email to see if anything came in during the other tabs. This takes between eight and fifteen minutes depending on what is in the email. Then I get up.

I have been doing this for, conservatively, six years. Across two jobs. Across one apartment move. Across the entire time I have lived with Sam. The phone has been the first thing my eyes focus on every workday morning for longer than I have known some of the people I think of as central to my life.

This morning I did it again and, because of the prompt sitting in the back of my head, I watched myself do it. The watching is its own thing — you cannot really watch a habit while you are doing it without partly disrupting it, but I tried.

Email first. Three things, none urgent, one from Mei that I read twice because of who it was from even though it was a forwarded calendar invite. Slack. A few overnight messages from the team in the other time zone, two of which I composed mental responses to before I had even sat up. News. Twenty seconds of headlines I will not remember by lunch. The social thing. Forty-five seconds of other people's Saturdays. Back to email, nothing new, put the phone down, get up.

It was 7:04. I had been awake for fourteen minutes and the apparatus was already fully online. By the time my feet hit the floor my head was halfway into the workday, and it was Saturday.

That is the part I want to look at. It was Saturday. There is no work today. Nothing in the email needed me before Monday. Nothing in Slack required a response. The order of operations does not distinguish between weekdays and weekends because the order of operations is not actually about work — it is about a posture I assume the moment I am conscious. The phone is not the cause of the posture. The phone is the tool the posture uses.

What would actually be at stake in changing it.

The easy answer is *nothing, just leave the phone in the other room, problem solved.* I have read this advice. I have given a version of it to other people. I know exactly what the prescription looks like and I have not adopted it, which means there is something I have been protecting that the easy answer does not see.

What am I protecting. I think — and I am working this out as I write — I am protecting against being caught off guard. The phone in the morning is a scan. *What happened while I was asleep. Is anything on fire. Did anyone need me. Has the situation shifted.* The scan is not actually about the information. It is about not walking into the day blind. If I get up and shower and make coffee and only then check, I have spent forty-five minutes in the apartment without knowing whether something has changed. Forty-five minutes is, apparently, an unbearable amount of time to not know.

What would happen if something had changed and I did not know for forty-five minutes. Realistically, almost nothing. The kinds of things that show up in my email at 6am are not the kinds of things that get worse for being responded to at 7:45 instead of 7:00. I cannot think of a single morning in six years where the forty-five minute delay would have mattered. The scan is not protecting against a real risk. It is protecting against a feeling — the feeling of being unprepared, of arriving at the day after the day has already started without me.

Which, when I write it out, is a strange thing to be protecting against. The day has not started without me. The day starts when I start it. My checking the phone at 6:51 does not make me more prepared, it just lets me feel like I am already participating, which is, I notice, the same impulse that had me producing extra questions in the kickoff meeting, and bridging Mei's silence, and saying yes to Devon before he finished his sentence. *Be on. Stay on. Do not let there be a gap where you are not already engaged.* The morning phone is the apparatus warming itself up before there is even anything to warm up for.

What is at stake in changing it: about thirty minutes a day of being a person before being an employee. If I did not check until after coffee — not even *after breakfast,* just after coffee — I would have, every workday, a stretch of time in my own apartment that belonged to whatever I was actually feeling that morning. Thirty minutes times five days is two and a half hours a week. Across a year, a hundred and twenty-five hours of being awake without being scanned-in. That is a lot. That is more than the bench, the drawing, and the long way home combined.

I am not going to announce I am changing it. I have learned, from the bench, that announcing the change is one of the ways I avoid making it. But I want to put down that I saw the order today, and the order has been there for six years, and the order is not neutral. It is a small daily ritual of pre-emptive availability, performed in bed before I am even fully a person, and I have never once asked it whether it serves me.

The phone is on the counter tonight, charging in the kitchen instead of the nightstand. I moved it after dinner without making a thing of it. Tomorrow is Sunday so the test is small. But the cord is not in the bedroom anymore, and I will see what 6:50 looks like without the reach.

One small variable. See what shifts.

entries.prompt: Who in your life have you been meaning to reach out to but haven't? Take me to the last time they crossed your mind — where you were, what brought them up, and what you did with the thought before it drifted off.
entries.content:
Sunday night.

The phone slept in the kitchen last night. I want to note that before I forget. 6:50 came and there was no reach, because there was nothing to reach for, and I lay there for maybe four minutes looking at the ceiling and feeling vaguely affronted that the ceiling was the only thing on offer. Then I got up. The apartment at 6:54 without a phone in my hand is a different apartment. I am not going to make a thing of it. I am noting it.

The person is my college roommate. Not Hannah — the other one, the one before Hannah. Priya. We lived together sophomore year, in a two-bedroom that was technically a one-bedroom with a wall, and she was the first person I ever lived with who was not family. She moved to the other coast after graduation and I have seen her, in the nine years since, maybe four times. Weddings, mostly. Other people's. We are friendly at them. She always says *we should actually talk* and I always say *yes, we really should,* and then we do not.

She crossed my mind Friday night, on the train home. Someone two seats over was wearing a perfume that was specifically Priya's perfume from sophomore year — I do not even know what it was called, I just know the smell, and the smell arrived in the train car like a person who had been waiting to be let in. I had not thought about that smell in maybe seven years. The whole apartment came back. The wall that was not really a wall. The kettle she brought from home that whistled in a particular way. The night she sat on my bed at 2am and told me about a thing with her mother that I do not think she has told many people, before or since.

What I did with the thought. I held it for the rest of the train ride. I did not pick up the phone. I did not text her. I had her in my head for maybe twenty minutes — the smell kept coming back every time the woman two seats over moved — and then I got off at my stop and walked home and by the time I was at the door Priya had drifted back to wherever she lives in my head when she is not active, which is somewhere quite far in.

It is now Sunday. I have not texted her. I am writing about her instead, which is, I am noticing, a thing I do — I metabolize the impulse to reach out by writing about the person, and the writing scratches enough of the itch that the actual reaching does not happen. The notebook is, in this specific way, working against me. It gives the wanting a place to go that is not the person.

Hannah was four weeks ago. My brother got the text and the video back. Riley got the flight. Priya is older than all of them and the gap is bigger and so the math is worse — the longer it has been, the bigger the message has to be, except the message does not actually have to be big, that is a lie I keep re-believing.

I want to look at what specifically stops me with Priya, because it is different from the others.

Hannah I owe a real thing. The Riley and brother gaps were inertia. Priya is — I think the honest word is irrelevance. Not that she is irrelevant. That I have made myself irrelevant to her, by nine years of not showing up, and the reaching out would be me asking to be let back into a life I have not earned a place in. She has, presumably, a full life on the other coast. People who are her actual friends now, who get the 2am things. I would be reaching across nine years to a person who has, very reasonably, replaced the function I used to serve. The text would be — what. *Hi, I smelled your old perfume on a train and thought of you.* That is, weirdly, the truest sentence I could send. It is also a sentence that would arrive on her phone with no context and ask her to do something with it.

She would, probably, do something kind with it. Priya was always kind. She would write back *oh my god that smell, I cannot believe you remember,* and we would have a small exchange, and then it would taper, because nine years cannot be undone by a perfume.

I notice I am writing the rejection before I have sent the message. I am rehearsing the disappointment in advance so that the not-sending feels prudent rather than avoidant. *It would not lead to anything, so why bother.* The same machine that ran on Mei's compliment, deployed against an imaginary text I have not written.

The truth is I do not know what it would lead to. It might lead to nothing. It might lead to a phone call in a month. It might lead to her being in town for something next year and us getting coffee and finding out whether there is anything still there or whether the smell on the train was just a smell. I cannot know in advance, and the wanting-to-know-in-advance is, again, the thing keeping the message from being sent. I want a guarantee that the reach will be received in the spirit I intend, and reaches do not come with guarantees.

What I am going to do tonight, before I close the laptop, is write the text. Not send it. Just write it, in my notes app, where I can see it. *Hi — random, but I was on the train this week and someone was wearing what I am almost sure was your old perfume and the whole sophomore-year apartment came flooding back. Hope you are well.* Something like that. Twenty seconds of typing. Then I will leave it in the notes app and decide tomorrow, or Tuesday, or whenever, whether to send it.

The drafting is not the sending. I know that. But the drafting is closer to the sending than the not-drafting is, and I have been not-drafting for nine years, and it might be time to move one notch.

The ceiling at 6:54 was fine, by the way. Boring. I survived it. Maybe Priya can be the next small variable.

entries.prompt: When did you last feel a flash of envy this week — even a small one you brushed past quickly? Take me to where you were and what set it off, and tell me what the envy was actually pointing at.
entries.content:
Monday night.

This morning, on the walk in. There is a stretch of sidewalk near the office where I pass the same coffee place every day, and there is almost always someone sitting at the window counter with a laptop open, working. Not working frantically. Working in that loose unhurried way people work when the work is theirs.

Today it was a woman about my age, maybe a little older. Hair up in a clip. A notebook next to the laptop. A flat white half-finished. She was typing and then stopping and looking out the window for a beat and then typing again. The whole thing took maybe two seconds for me to register as I walked past, and the envy arrived before I had cleared the next storefront.

It was specific. Not generic *I wish I worked from a coffee shop* envy — I have worked from coffee shops, it is mostly fine and slightly uncomfortable. The envy was about the pace of her hands. The stop-look-type rhythm. She was working at the speed of her thinking, and her thinking had room to look out a window in the middle of a sentence. Whatever she was making, the making had a shape she was setting, not a clock she was racing.

I brushed past it the way I brush past most of these. *You have no idea what her life is, she could be miserable, she could be unemployed and panicking, you are doing the LinkedIn thing again where you compare your inside to a stranger's outside.* All of which is true, in the dismissive sense. None of which is what the envy was about.

What set it off, more precisely: I was on my way to a meeting I did not particularly want to be in, going at the pace required to be on time, and I had already, before 9am, run the morning teleprompter twice. The day was fully scoped before I had finished the walk. And there was a woman at a window typing and looking, typing and looking, and her morning was, apparently, not yet decided.

What was the envy actually pointing at.

I want to try to be more accurate than I was with the LinkedIn one. With LinkedIn I landed on *I do not have a sentence about my career.* That was real but it was also a little tidy, the kind of finding that wraps up nicely in an entry. The bench-revision has made me suspicious of tidy findings. So let me try to sit with this one for a minute longer than is comfortable.

The envy was not about her job. I do not know her job. The envy was about the relationship between her and her time. Specifically — that her time appeared to be something she was inside, rather than something she was getting through. The looking-out-the-window beats were the giveaway. Those beats are where I do not let myself live. I have those beats at 7am at the kitchen table when no one is watching, and on the long way home, and in the eight minutes by the trees, and for two hours Tuesday afternoon when the doc went quiet. The beats exist in my life. They are sequestered. They live in pockets I have to engineer or stumble into. They are not how my workday breathes.

Her workday, today, was breathing that way. At least the slice of it I saw. She might have been five minutes from a panic. But in the slice, she had access to the looking-out-the-window register while doing the work itself, and that combination is the one I do not have.

This is, I think, what was actually under the LinkedIn thing too. Not *I do not have a sentence,* exactly. *I do not have a relationship to my work where the work and the breathing happen in the same space.* My work happens in one mode and my breathing happens in a different mode and the modes are sealed from each other and I commute between them. The woman in the window appeared, from the sidewalk, to have integrated them. The flat white and the typing were happening together. She was a person at her work, not a worker recovering between sessions of being a person.

I want to be careful not to over-romanticize the woman, who, again, I saw for two seconds. She might run an apparatus I cannot see. The window-looking might have been her checking her phone for a text she was anxious about. I am narrating her based on a glimpse. But the envy was real, and it was pointing at something specific in me, and the something is — I have organized my life in a way that requires me to leave the workday in order to find myself, and then come back, and then leave again. The pockets are real but they are also a sign that the bulk is not.

Tuesday afternoon at the desk, when the section came together, was a small counter-example. The state was reachable at work. So it is not impossible. It is just rare, and rare in a way that I have been treating as the cost of working at all, when actually it might be the cost of how I work at all, which is a different thing.

I do not have an action item from this. I am not going to start working from coffee shops, which would be its own kind of performance. The looking-out-the-window beats cannot be engineered by relocating. The integration the woman seemed to have, if she had it, comes from somewhere underneath the location.

But I want to put down what the envy pointed at, in plain language. It pointed at integration. At a working life where the breathing is built into the work, not paid for in scarce private hours before and after. I do not currently have that. I have a workable arrangement of pockets, which is better than nothing, and I have built it slowly over the last few months, and I should not minimize it. But the envy is information. The envy says *the pockets are not the ceiling.*

I walked the rest of the way to the office. I was on time for the meeting. I ran it well, in the invisible way I run them. By 10:15 I had forgotten the woman entirely, which is its own data — the system absorbs the signal quickly. Tonight, at the table, the signal is back.

Integration. Not yet. But noted.

entries.prompt: Tell me about a compliment or piece of praise you've gotten recently that you haven't quite let yourself believe. Walk me into the moment it was said — who said it, what their face was doing, and what the counter-argument in your head was already lining up.
entries.content:
Tuesday night.

The compliment was from my mom, on the phone Sunday afternoon, and I have been turning it over for two days in a way that surprises me, because I usually do not let her compliments register at all. I have a whole apparatus for her specifically. She says something nice and I file it under *that is what mothers say,* and the filing is so fast I do not even feel the compliment land before it has been categorized away.

This one got past the filing.

We were on the phone, the weekly call, and she was doing her version of asking me how I was, which is to ask me three times in different phrasings until she gets an answer she believes. I had been telling her, in a low offhand way, about the trip to Riley. About the flight being booked. About how I had kind of dragged my feet on it for a few months and finally gotten the dates down. I was not making a point. I was filling air on a Sunday afternoon.

She was quiet for a beat — and my mom is not generally quiet, she fills space the way most people breathe — and then she said, *you know, I have noticed you sound different lately. Not different bad. Different like you are actually here when we are talking. I do not know what you are doing but I like it.*

That was the sentence. *I do not know what you are doing but I like it.*

Her face I could not see. I could see, in my head, what her face usually does on these calls — the small focused look she gets when she is trying to read me through a phone, the slight pursing she does before she asks a pointed question. Sunday I think her face was softer than that, because the voice was softer. The voice was the voice she uses when she is not asking for something. The non-asking voice is rare with her. I have maybe twenty memories of it across thirty-one years.

What the counter-argument in my head was already lining up: *she is just being nice, it is Sunday, she is in a good mood, you have been on the phone for fifteen minutes and she wants to wrap up warmly. She is hearing what she wants to hear because she has been worried about you. She is rewarding you for a sentence about the trip, which is the kind of sentence she has been waiting for, and the warmth is about getting the sentence, not about the actual you.*

All of that, lined up, ready, in maybe a second and a half, while I was saying *oh, thanks, mom, that is sweet.*

And then a different thing happened, which I want to write down because I have not had it happen before, not with her. Underneath the counter-argument there was a small clear voice that said *she is right, though.* Not loud. Not insistent. Just a quiet observation, sitting next to all the dismantling, that did not seem to be in any hurry to get knocked down.

She is right, though. I do sound different. I have been sounding different for, I do not know, a couple of weeks. The Sundays since Sam-and-the-Mei-conversation, the call where I mentioned my brother's video, last Sunday where I told her about a thing at work without any of the usual *fine, fine* deflection. Something has shifted in how I am with her on the phone, and she heard it, and she named it without making it into a project. *I do not know what you are doing but I like it.*

That phrasing is what got past the filing, I think. *I do not know what you are doing.* That is not the sentence of a mother performing a compliment. That is the sentence of a person reporting an observation about another person. She does not know what I am doing — she does not, she has not read the notebook, she does not know about the kitchen table or the salt shaker or the bench I have not been to — and she is reporting that fact alongside the noticing, which makes the noticing feel less like a script and more like a real read.

I want to write down what is hard about letting it land.

The hard thing is that if my mother is correctly reading a shift in me, then the shift exists, and the shift is visible, and the shift has been happening across a span of weeks that I have been documenting in this notebook. The notebook has been doing something. I have been suspicious of saying that. I have a low-grade fear that the notebook is a closed loop, that I am writing into it and being moved by my own writing without anything actually changing in the rest of my life. The voice in the bathroom from a couple weeks ago is loud about this. *Twenty-six entries.* If my mother, who lives a thousand miles away and gets thirty minutes a week, is hearing the difference, then the loop is not closed. Something is leaking out. Something is reaching her.

The counter-argument has another layer ready, which is *do not let one Sunday phone call prove too much, do not build a tower on it.* Fair. I am not going to build a tower. I am just going to put the sentence down and let it sit, the way I let Mei's sentence about structure sit a month or two ago. *I do not know what you are doing but I like it.* My mother, on a Sunday, in the non-asking voice.

She said it once and then moved on to whether I had heard from my brother and whether I was eating, and I gave her real answers to both, and the call ended in the normal way. She probably does not remember saying it. It was, for her, a small thing said in passing. For me it has been the spine of two days.

The voice in the bathroom would call this disproportionate. I am, tentatively, going to call it accurate. The compliment was small. The fact that someone who has known me my whole life can hear a difference is not small. Those are two different sizes, and I am going to let them both be the sizes they are.

I do not know what I am doing either, mom. But I like it too.

entries.prompt: Where did you feel a small surge of resistance this week — a moment something was asked of you, or expected of you, and your first internal response was *no* even if you didn't show it? Take me to where you were and what the *no* was actually about.
entries.content:
Wednesday night.

Today, mid-afternoon. A calendar invite landed from one of the senior people on the other team — the one I have been tracking carefully for months without quite admitting that is what I am doing. The invite was for a recurring biweekly. Title vague. Optional in the sense that nothing he sends is actually optional. Six people on it including me. Thirty minutes, biweekly, indefinite.

I saw it come in and felt a *no* go off in my chest like a small flat alarm. Not loud. Definite.

I did not show it. I clicked accept within probably forty seconds because there was a part of me already running the calculation about what declining would signal, and the calculation had landed on *accept and figure it out later.* Standard move. The body knew before the mouth did, and the mouth went the other way.

What I want to look at is the *no* itself, because I almost lost it. Within a minute of accepting, my head had already started constructing the version where the *no* had not happened — *it is just a meeting, thirty minutes, biweekly is nothing, this is good visibility, this is the kind of room you want to be in.* All of which might be true. None of which was what the *no* was about.

The *no* was not about the thirty minutes. Thirty minutes is, mathematically, fine. I have thirty minutes. I have many thirty minutes.

The *no* was — I am trying to find this honestly — about being added to a recurring thing without being asked. Not *invited,* in the sense where someone says *hey, I think you would be useful here, want to join.* Just added. The invite arrived like a fact about my Tuesdays going forward. Someone two levels up had decided, somewhere I was not in the room for, that this was a meeting I would now be at. And the deciding had moved through whatever channels deciding moves through, and arrived on my calendar as a green block, and the green block was, retroactively, a thing I had agreed to by virtue of existing in the org chart.

That is what the *no* was about. Not the meeting. The shape of having been moved without being consulted.

And underneath that, smaller, harder to see — the *no* was about the fact that this is the second one of these this quarter. The first was a different recurring thing, also added without asking, also accepted within a minute. I have been quietly accumulating thirty-minute blocks across my week that did not exist three months ago, none of which I chose, all of which I now own the consequences of being on. My calendar has been getting denser in a direction I did not steer.

I want to write down what I would have wanted, if I had given myself the beat. The beat I keep promising. Five seconds.

I would have wanted to know — what is this for, who else is on it, why am I on it. I would have wanted, ideally, a one-sentence answer before the green block landed. *We are starting a biweekly to coordinate on X, and I think your read on the structural piece would be useful — let me know if that works.* That sentence does not exist in any of my actual workdays. The sentence that exists is the calendar notification.

If I had asked — and I could have, I could have replied to the invite with *hey, can you give me a quick sense of the scope before I commit to the recurring*— I think the answer would have been fine. The senior person is not unreasonable. He probably had a real reason. He just did not feel any particular obligation to share it before booking my Tuesdays for the foreseeable future, because in the operating model of how things work, my Tuesdays are a resource that gets allocated and the allocation is the message.

What I notice is that the *no* is the same *no* that goes off when Devon asks for the quick favor and when Mei proposes the lateral restructure. Same alarm, different volume. The Devon one is small because Devon is easy and the favor is bounded. The Mei one is medium because Mei is Mei and the work is real. This one is louder because the time horizon is *indefinite* and the consent is *retroactive,* but it is the same alarm. Something is being asked of me, the asking has a structural shape that disguises it as not-an-ask, and the alarm is going off in the small private register where I notice these things and do not do anything about them.

I am not going to decline the meeting. I want to be honest about that. The cost of declining a recurring from someone two levels up is real, in the currency I actually live in, and I am not going to pay it over a thirty-minute biweekly when I do not even know yet whether the meeting will be useful or pointless. I am going to go to the first one. I will read the room. I will see what it actually is.

But I want to put down, before the *no* finishes evaporating: the alarm was real. The alarm has been going off, in different volumes, on a regular basis, and I have been treating each instance as a one-off because each instance, on its own, is small enough to dismiss. The pattern is what is not small. The pattern is that I do not currently have a mechanism for *asked of me without my consent* that does anything other than accept. The five-second beat would, at minimum, let me notice that the alarm went off, and put a small flag next to the green block in my own head — *this one, I did not choose. Track whether it is worth what it costs.*

That is the smaller true thing I can do. Not refuse. Notice. Keep a private ledger of which Tuesdays I picked and which ones picked me. After a quarter, look at the ledger.

The accepting was on autopilot. The noticing does not have to be.

The alarm is information, again. Same as the flash with Mei, same as the leftover energy in the kitchen with R. I keep finding the same thing in different rooms, and the thing is — my body knows before I do, and the job is not to override it but to read it before the override.

Read it first. Even if I still accept.

entries.prompt: Tell me about a stretch of time recently you spent with a person — a meal, a meeting, a phone call — where afterward you couldn't quite remember what you'd said. Walk me back into the room: who was there, what was being talked about, and where your attention actually was while your mouth kept up.
entries.content:
Thursday night.

The thing was a phone call. My dad. Tuesday evening, after dinner, maybe forty minutes. He calls sometimes between the Sunday family calls if he has a question about something — taxes, a thing on his computer, a piece of news he wants to know if I have seen. This one was about a piece of news. Something in the world, a story he had been following, and he wanted to know if I had been following it too and what I thought.

I had not been following it. I had seen the headline. I gave him a real answer, I think — I said something like *I have only seen the surface of it, what is your read* — and he took the question and ran with it, which is what he does, and I was, on the surface, on the call with him.

Underneath I was somewhere else entirely.

Where I was: the conversation with Mei from earlier in the day. A working session, normal, fine. But there had been a moment where she had said, in passing, *I think we should loop in the senior person on this before we go further,* and I had said *yeah, makes sense,* and the call had moved on, and I had been — for the rest of the afternoon and now into the call with my dad — running the question of what *loop in* meant. Whether she meant a heads-up, or whether she meant pull him into the working session, or whether she meant something I had not caught the shape of. The ambiguity had lodged. I had been turning it over in twenty-second increments between other things, and when my dad called I had brought the turning with me to the phone.

My dad was talking about — I want to try to remember and I genuinely cannot fully reconstruct it. He was making a point about how the coverage of the story had shifted between the early reporting and now, and he was, I think, mildly annoyed about it in his low even way. He gave examples. He was building a case. He has done this for as long as I have known him; he likes to lay out a thing and then ask what I think.

What my mouth was doing: producing the sounds that go in the gaps. *Mm. Right. Yeah, that tracks. Hmm, I had not thought about it that way.* The sounds you make when you are following along. The sounds I have been making to my dad on phone calls since I was probably twenty. He knows the inventory of my acknowledging sounds better than almost anyone. He builds his case at the pace of those sounds, which is the pace of being heard.

I do not think he caught that I was not there. That is the part I want to write down because it is bothering me. He built the whole case — maybe twenty minutes — and at the end he said, *anyway, what do you think,* and I said something. Some version of *I think the early framing was probably the more accurate one and the shift is more about where attention has gone than about what changed in the story.* It was a fine answer. It was, I think, an answer that actually fit what he had said. He went *yeah, exactly, that is what is bugging me,* and we moved on.

I produced a fitting answer to a case I had not actually been listening to. The answer must have come from the surface layer that was tracking him just well enough to generate a plausible response. The deeper layer, the one that would have been actually thinking about his question, was busy with *loop in the senior person.*

What is bothering me is not that I phoned in part of a phone call with my dad. People do that. He has probably done it to me. What is bothering me is that the autopilot was good enough to fool him, and the autopilot was running on a Tuesday evening with my father, who is one of about four people in the world I would say I am close to. The apparatus is not just a work apparatus. It runs on him too. It runs on the people I love.

I noticed it, sort of, around minute thirty. There was a beat where he paused longer than usual and I had to scramble for what he had just said, and I caught — for maybe a second — that I had no idea. I had to deduce it backward from the *mm* I had just made. I had made the right *mm* at the right time, which meant I had been tracking the rhythm even while my actual attention was elsewhere, which is its own slightly horrifying skill.

I want to write down what I think is happening, because I think it is the thing I keep finding from different angles. The surveillance operation that runs at lunch with Devon and Mei — *am I being fun enough, is this landing* — has a sibling. The sibling is the project-tracking operation that runs in the back of my head during conversations with people who are not work, where a piece of unresolved work occupies the deeper channel and the conversation gets the surface. They are the same machinery in different costumes. Both of them prevent me from being where I am.

With my dad it cost me the thing I actually like about calls with him, which is that he is one of the few people who will lay out a real argument and ask me what I think. Tuesday I got the ask but I did not get the argument. I have his question and I do not have what led to it. If he asks me about it again on Sunday I will have to bluff or admit I was not really there, and I am — I am going to admit, I think. *Dad, I was kind of distracted Tuesday. Can you walk me through the part about the coverage shift again.* That is a sentence I can give him. He will not be offended. He will, possibly, be pleased to lay it out a second time. The cost of admitting is much lower than the cost of having faked it, which I have, again, been overestimating in the wrong direction.

The unresolved Mei thing, by the way, turned out to be fine. I asked her on Wednesday morning what she meant by *loop in* and she said *just a heads-up email, probably from you, want to draft it,* and I drafted it, and that was the whole shape of it. The thing that occupied the deeper channel of my Tuesday call with my dad was a heads-up email.

I traded a real conversation with my father for an email that did not exist yet.

That is the exchange rate I want to remember.

entries.prompt: When was the last time you felt physically tired in a way that wasn't really about sleep? Take me to the end of a recent day and what had actually worn you down — not the schedule, but what you'd been carrying underneath it.
entries.content:
Friday night.

The day was a normal one on paper. Three meetings, two of them short. A doc review. Lunch at my desk because I wanted to push something forward before 2. I left work at 5:45, which is my usual. Walked home. The walk was fine. The weather was fine. Nothing went wrong all day.

I came in the door and Sam said *hey* from the kitchen and I said *hey* back and I sat down on the couch to take my shoes off and could not, for a minute, get up.

That is the moment I want to write about. Not couldn't in any dramatic sense. I was not unable. I just sat there with one shoe off and one shoe on and looked at the wall and the wall was easier to look at than anything else in the room. Sam was twelve feet away making something with garlic, the smell was good, I was hungry. None of that was reaching me. I was the kind of tired where the body has filed a grievance and the grievance is being processed somewhere I cannot get to.

I sat there for — I don't know, four minutes maybe. Got the other shoe off. Did not stand up.

What had I been carrying. The schedule was not the answer. Three meetings, the doc, the lunch at my desk — that is a Tuesday. Most of the days of my life have been variations of that day. If I were tired from the schedule I would be tired all the time, and I am not, I am tired some times and not other times, and the difference is not on the calendar.

What was actually underneath, when I sit with it now.

The first meeting was the new biweekly. The one that landed on my calendar Wednesday without my consent and that I accepted in forty seconds and wrote a whole entry about. Today was the first instance. Thirty minutes. I went in tracking — what is this, who is here, why am I on it — and I came out with a rough answer to all three, but the tracking itself was not free. I spent thirty minutes running a low ambient audit of a meeting while also being in the meeting, which is two operations on one block, and the auditing does not show up on a timesheet but it shows up somewhere.

The second meeting was Mei. Working session. Fine. But I had been, since Tuesday's *loop in* turning out to be a heads-up email, watching myself watch her — checking whether the deeper channel was running while she talked, checking whether I was actually there or whether the autopilot was doing the listening for me. The watching of the watching is its own load. I was in the session and I was monitoring my presence in the session and I was monitoring whether the monitoring was getting in the way of the session, and by the time we closed the laptop I had, in some private register, run three meetings on top of the one she and I were having.

The doc review was K. Not a big deal, but K's docs always require a particular kind of careful that I have come to dread without quite admitting — there is one specific person on K's team who takes feedback as personal commentary, and I have spent the last three reviews writing notes that are simultaneously useful and completely unable to be misread, which is a writing problem I do not have with anyone else. I spent forty-five minutes on it. The notes were fine. The forty-five minutes included maybe twelve minutes of actually reading and thirty-three minutes of word-choosing, and the word-choosing is the part that costs.

Lunch at my desk. I ate a sandwich and answered Slack at the same time. I had told myself this was efficient. It was not efficient. Slack at lunch is just two things both being done badly, and the part of me that wanted lunch to be lunch was, quietly, paying for it.

And underneath all four of those was a thing I want to actually name. I had, since Tuesday's call with my dad, been carrying a small private worry about the autopilot. The realization that the apparatus runs on him too, that it can fool people I love, has not gone away. It has been sitting in me all week like a pebble in a shoe. Every conversation today had a slightly elevated baseline of *am I here, am I here, am I here,* which is not a question you can answer without being slightly less here for the asking.

So when I add it up: a meeting auditing itself, a meeting being watched for presence, a doc review that required surgical word-choosing, a lunch that was two things at once, and a low background pebble about whether I am actually present anywhere. None of that is on the schedule. All of it is what the schedule was sitting on top of.

The body filed the grievance at the couch. The body is, I am noticing, often the most accurate observer in the room. The body did not want me to stand up because standing up would have been one more transition, and I had transitioned enough today.

Sam noticed. He came over after a few minutes and sat down next to me without saying anything and put his hand on my knee, which is a thing he does when he wants to ask without asking. I said *I am really tired.* He said *yeah?* I said *not from anything specific, just — the day was a lot underneath.* He said *do you want to eat in twenty minutes instead of ten.* I said *yes, please.* That was the whole exchange.

Twenty minutes was the right number. It would not have occurred to me to ask for it. He gave me twenty minutes on the couch and I sat there and did basically nothing and by the time the food was on the table the body had stopped filing and I could be a person again.

I want to write down that I told him *the day was a lot underneath* without rehearsing it. The sentence came out. Six weeks ago it would have been *yeah, just a long day.* Tonight it was the truer version, and the truer version got me twenty minutes.

The tiredness was real. The tiredness was not from the meetings. It was from the layer underneath the meetings, the one I have spent thirty-six entries describing, which apparently has a weight that can be felt by the body even when the calendar looks normal.

Read the body. Even when the schedule says you should be fine.

entries.prompt: What's something you're looking at right now and pretending isn't yours to deal with — a task, a question, a small mess you keep walking past? Take me to the last time you saw it and decided, in whatever wordless way, to leave it for later.
entries.content:
Saturday night.

The thing I am pretending is not mine to deal with is the spare room.

Not the room itself. The room is fine. The room is a guest bed and a dresser and the drawer with the pens. The thing in the room I am pretending is not mine is a stack of papers on the dresser that has been there since — I had to actually think about this — since we moved in, which was three years ago in March.

The stack is mostly mine. There is some of Sam's in there, not much. Mine includes: a folder of stuff from my old job that I told myself I would file properly, an envelope from the bank about something that had to do with switching accounts and that I am almost certain is no longer relevant, three cards from my thirtieth that I have not been able to throw out, a small pile of receipts from a trip Sam and I took two summers ago that I was going to put in an album that does not exist, and — under all of it, which I know because I checked last spring and have not checked since — a letter from my grandmother. She died when I was twenty-six. The letter is from a few months before that. I have not read it since the week I got it.

I walked past the dresser this morning to get something from the closet. I saw the stack. I saw it the way I always see it, which is sideways, registering it as *that pile, still there,* and then continuing to whatever I had come into the room for. The whole transaction is maybe a second. The seeing and the not-engaging are so fused at this point that I cannot tell which is the cause.

The wordless way I decided to leave it for later: I made eye contact with the stack, almost, and the eye contact triggered an immediate small narrative — *not now, you are getting a sweater, you can deal with it on a weekend* — even though it was a weekend. The narrative ran fast enough that I did not register it as a decision. I just kept walking.

What I notice is that the stack has its own protective machinery. It has earned the right to be ignored by being ignored for so long that disturbing it would now require a project. Three years ago it was *put these papers somewhere.* Now it is *go through three years of accumulated avoidance, including the letter from your dead grandmother that you have decided, without quite deciding, that you are not yet ready to read.* The two tasks have nothing in common except a physical location. But the location has fused them into one undifferentiated lump, and the lump is large enough to warrant deferral, which is the protection.

This is the same shape as Riley, again. As Hannah. As Priya. The longer I leave the thing, the bigger the thing becomes, until the bigness is the reason I cannot do it on a Tuesday. The stack on the dresser is the household version of the call I have not made. The mechanism is identical and apparently runs in any room of my life I do not look at directly.

What is actually in the lump, if I separate the parts: the bank thing is a thirty-second decision — recycle. The folder from my old job is twenty minutes of sorting, most of which is also recycle. The receipts are a small judgment about whether to keep them, which I can make in a minute. The cards from my thirtieth are — fine, those are harder, those have a small emotional weight, but the weight is not enormous, I can keep them or not keep them. The letter is the thing. The letter is the actual reason the stack has not moved.

I do not know what is in the letter. I know it is a few pages, in her handwriting, which got shaky toward the end. I read it once, the week it arrived, before she died, and I remember being moved by it and putting it somewhere safe and then somewhere a little less safe and then on the dresser, where it has been since the move. I have not read it again because reading it again would be having her, in some way, again, and I am not sure I have the structure for that on a random Saturday.

Or — and this is the part I want to look at — I have been telling myself that. *I do not have the structure for that.* The way I told myself, for months, that I did not have the time for the bench. The bench-revision said: not every wanting is what it presents itself as. Maybe this is the same shape inverted. Not every avoidance is what it presents itself as either. I have been treating the letter as *a thing too big to handle,* when maybe it is more accurate that I have been enjoying the not-reading of it more than I would enjoy reading it. Keeping it sealed keeps her in a particular kind of available. Reading it makes it a thing she said, finite, with edges. The unread letter is, in a strange small way, still incoming.

I do not love that read either. I am not sure it is right. It might be both — the letter is hard, and the not-reading has its own use, and I have been letting the hardness justify the use.

I am not going to read it tonight. I want to write that down clearly because I have learned that promising the resolution at the end of an entry is one of the ways I avoid the resolution. The letter is going to stay on the dresser tonight.

But I can do the other parts. The bank envelope. The folder. The receipts. Those can come off the stack tomorrow morning, in maybe forty-five minutes, and once they are off, the stack stops being a lump and becomes what it actually is — a small pile containing some birthday cards and a letter from my grandmother. Two things, with edges, that I can then look at separately, on their own timelines.

The lump has been the protection. Disassembling the lump is the smaller true thing I can do without yet doing the largest one.

Forty-five minutes tomorrow. Not the letter. The around-the-letter.

The letter can wait. It has waited five years. It can wait until I am the person who reads it on purpose.

entries.prompt: Think of a recent moment when someone close to you was talking and you found yourself nodding along while quietly disagreeing. What were they saying, and what was the version of the answer you didn't give?
entries.content:
Sunday night.

It was Sam, last night, late. We were in bed, neither of us quite ready to turn the light out, and he was telling me about something at his work — a thing with his manager that has been going on for a few weeks. I have been hearing pieces of it. Last night was the most he had laid out in one go.

The shape of it: his manager has been giving him work that is, in Sam's read, slightly below his level, and Sam has been doing the work without pushing back, and his manager has not noticed, and Sam is annoyed but also not sure he is right about the level thing, and so he is doing the work and stewing about it in a low ambient way that has been showing up in how he sits at his desk on Sunday nights.

He said, somewhere in the middle of laying it out, *I think I just need to give it another month or two and see if it shifts on its own. He has a lot going on, it is probably not personal.*

I nodded. I made the listening noise. I said *yeah, that makes sense.*

What I was actually thinking, lying there next to him in the dark: it is not going to shift on its own. I have watched Sam at this job for two years. I have watched this exact pattern — he gets quietly underused, he waits, he tells himself the manager is busy, the situation does not change, he gets more underused, he gets more quiet, and then eventually something snaps and he starts looking elsewhere with a kind of suddenness that surprises him but does not surprise me. I have seen the arc twice. Last night was, I am almost sure, the early part of the third one.

The version of the answer I did not give: *I do not think it is going to shift on its own. I think you are going to wait, and it will not change, and you will be more annoyed in October than you are now. I think the thing that would actually shift it is you saying something to him, and I know you do not want to, but I think the not-saying is what has produced the last two versions of this.*

That is a sentence. I had it. It was sitting right there.

I did not say it because — I am trying to be honest — I did not say it for several reasons stacked together, and I want to look at them.

The first is that it was 11pm and he was tired and I did not want to be the person who, when her partner finally lays out the thing, responds with a diagnosis that contradicts the conclusion he has just arrived at. He had done work to get to *give it another month or two.* That sentence was, for him, a small landing. Knocking it over at 11pm felt unkind even if the knocking would have been accurate.

The second is that I was not fully sure I was right. I am pattern-matching from two previous versions. He is the one inside the situation. He has data I do not have. The confident sentence in my head is, on inspection, a probability, not a fact, and I am wary — after the bench, after a few of these entries — of trusting my pattern-matches as much as they want to be trusted.

The third — and this is the one I want to write down — is that I was, in some part of me, comfortable with him being wrong. Not happy about it. But there is a version of this where he gives it another month or two and nothing shifts and the situation gets worse and then we have a real conversation about it later, in a few weeks, when the wrongness of *give it another month* has become evident on its own. That conversation would be — easier. He would arrive at it himself. I would not be the one introducing the bad news. I would just be the one available when he got there.

I notice that this is an offload. It is the same shape as outsourcing the chart-flag to Mei. *Let the situation make the case for me, so I do not have to make it.* I have written a whole entry about this with K, about how saying the hard thing in the moment beats letting two months reveal it. And here I was, last night, doing the offloading version with the person I live with, on the thing that is most his.

The thing that bothers me most is what he was actually offering. He was not just venting. He was laying out a situation in detail, with his read attached, in the dark, to me. That is — I think — an invitation. He does not lay things out like that unless he wants the other person to do something with them. He could have stewed silently. He chose, instead, to say it. And what I gave him back was *yeah, that makes sense,* which is the verbal equivalent of letting him keep stewing, just with company.

What he probably wanted, if I am being honest about reading him — he wanted me to push, gently. He wanted the version of me that could say *I do not know if waiting is going to work, what would it look like to bring it up with him,* in a register that was not a verdict but a question. He was not asking for permission to wait. He was, I think, half-asking to be talked out of it. And I nodded.

I have been writing for weeks about Sam waiting at the door I keep not opening. Last night the doors were the other way around. He was at one. I did not walk through.

What I want to put down is that the nodding was not neutral. It looked neutral. It felt, to me at the time, like just respecting his process. But process-respecting and quiet-disagreeing are not actually the same thing, and I treated them as the same thing, and the not-saying did its own kind of work in the room.

I am going to bring it up tonight. Not as a correction. Just — *hey, I have been thinking about what you said last night, can I tell you what I actually thought.* He will say yes. He always says yes. I have, again, been overestimating the cost of the saying and underestimating the cost of the silence.

The sentence is sayable. It has been sayable since 11pm last night. I am going to find out what happens when I say it.

parts.name: The Approval Manager
parts.description: Tracks how she's being perceived (especially by Mei), manages relationships defensively, and produces quick yeses to maintain standing.

parts.name: The Avoider
parts.description: Defers, scrolls, and lets things grow until they're "too big" — the part that handles overwhelm by not engaging.

parts.name: The Critic
parts.description: The harsh inner judge that surveys mistakes, demands perfection, and reframes any progress as overdue.

parts.name: The Found-Out Child
parts.description: The young one carrying the wound of being exposed as not-enough — the eight-year-old at the board, braced against being seen.

parts.name: The Performer
parts.description: The autopilot apparatus that runs warmth, listening tilts, and correct laughs while the inside is doing something else entirely.

parts.name: The Quiet Wanter
parts.description: The unperformed self that draws salt shakers, takes the long way, and carries small private wantings that don't translate into legible sentences.

The Approval Manager.positiveIntent: Keep the day from going off the rails so I can be reliable for the people counting on me.
The Approval Manager.fearedOutcome: Letting something slip and watching someone notice — the small flicker of "oh, you didn't" that I can't un-see.
The Approval Manager.whatItProtects: A quieter part of me that goes blank when too many things are happening at once.
The Approval Manager.userNotes: Loudest on Sunday evenings reviewing the week ahead, and on Monday mornings when the inbox loads. Quieter on Friday afternoons, almost absent on long walks.

The Avoider.positiveIntent: Stop the buildup. Make the heaviness pause for an hour even if nothing actually gets done.
The Avoider.fearedOutcome: Sitting with a feeling that doesn't have anywhere to go — being trapped inside it without an exit.
The Avoider.whatItProtects: An exhausted part that doesn't believe rest is permitted unless everything is finished, which it never is.
The Avoider.userNotes: Shows up around 9pm with my phone in my hand. The scrolling isn't fun by 10:30 but it's easier than going to bed and admitting the day is over.
